title: A Queensland case study into what household data from the 15th of January 2026 tells us about home battery price responsiveness
author: Emma Dyson
classified_content: The summary of the news "A Queensland case study into what household data from the 15th of January 2026 tells us about home battery price responsiveness" posted at 29-04-2026 02:53:00 PM is: On the 15th of January 2026, a hot day in Queensland with temperatures reaching 31.5°C led to extreme wholesale price spikes exceeding $19,000/MWh during the evening peak. A case study using smart meter data compared households on major retailers versus those on spot-price exposed plans, with and without batteries. It found that customers on spot-price exposed plans with larger batteries actively discharged stored solar to the grid during the high-price periods, while major retailer customers used batteries primarily for self-consumption and did not export. The study highlights that both battery capacity and price signals influence household behaviour, particularly during evening peaks. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Single day (15 January 2026). Its root_cause is: High demand due to hot weather (31.5°C) causing supply-demand imbalance and extreme wholesale price spikes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 2026-01-15. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Residential customers (with and without batteries), major electricity retailers, and spot-price exposed retailers. Its cause type is: Market-driven price spike due to weather. Its causes is: Hot weather on 15 January 2026, high evening demand from air conditioning, limited supply or generation constraints leading to wholesale price spikes.

title: Almost two months without output at Hunter Power Station, and another tiny slip in the operational date
author: Dan Lee
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Almost two months without output at Hunter Power Station, and another tiny slip in the operational date" posted at 28-04-2026 03:31:00 PM is: The Hunter Power Station (formerly Kurri Kurri) has been without any grid output since the evening of Monday, 2nd March 2026, and its projected full operational date has slipped again to Tuesday, 26th May 2026, according to AEMO’s latest MTPASA run. Commissioning testing was paused in late March, with Snowy Hydro citing uncertainty over when to complete diesel testing given the station is expected to be rarely needed. The cumulative delays continue to extend the plant's unavailability, reducing planned capacity additions in the National Electricity Market.

Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).  
Its category is: market reactions (as the delays affect supply expectations and market projections).  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 2nd March 2026 (when output ceased) until projected return on 26th May 2026 (ongoing).  
Its root_cause is: commissioning delays and a pause in diesel testing due to Snowy Hydro's reassessment of when testing is appropriate.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported; only a commissioning delay).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Key event dates: 2nd March 2026 (output ceased), 25th March 2026 (testing paused announced), 26th May 2026 (new projected full availability).  
Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (Hunter region, part of the NEM).  
Its affected key users is: AEMO (market operator), Snowy Hydro (owner/operator), and electricity consumers in the NEM.  
Its cause type is: commissioning delay / testing pause.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is:  
- Output from both units stopped on 2nd March 2026.  
- On 25th March 2026, Snowy Hydro paused testing and delayed the operational date, stating it is considering when to complete diesel testing.  
- Continued slippage in AEMO’s MTPASA projections, with the latest forecast pushing full availability to 26th May 2026.

title: Unit 2 out of service for over a week, following fire (during maintenance) at Bairnsdale Power Station
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unit 2 out of service for over a week, following fire (during maintenance) at Bairnsdale Power Station" posted at 28-04-2026 01:21:00 PM is: A fire broke out during maintenance at Bairnsdale Power Station's Unit 2 on the evening of April 27, 2026, causing the unit to be unavailable for at least a week until May 7, 2026, as shown in AEMO's PASA data. Unit 1 experienced only a short outage until April 29, 2026. The fire prompted an unexpected generator outage, reducing available capacity in the National Electricity Market. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Approximately 10 days (from April 27 to May 7, 2026). Its root_cause is: Fire during maintenance at Bairnsdale Power Station. Its accident scale is: Single unit (Unit 2) fire; Unit 1 short outage. Its dates is: Event date: 27-04-2026; Article date: 28-04-2026. Its affected region is: Bairnsdale Power Station, National Electricity Market (NEM), likely Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (NEM market participants and electricity consumers). Its cause type is: Fire (during maintenance). Its causes is: Maintenance activity at Bairnsdale Power Station ignited a fire, leading to Unit 2's extended outage.

title: One image to summarise the sequence of events in the Frequency Spikes of Fri 10th and Sat 11th April 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "One image to summarise the sequence of events in the Frequency Spikes of Fri 10th and Sat 11th April 2026" posted at 26-04-2026 05:50:00 PM is: The article describes two near-identical frequency spikes in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) occurring on 10 and 11 April 2026, caused primarily by the behavior of semi-scheduled units across three dispatch intervals. The events remain under analysis 16 and 15 days later, with a single image summarizing the sequence for a number of those units, highlighting their role in the grid instability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (immediate grid instability, with continued analytical interest over weeks). Its root_cause is: erratic behavior and availability issues of semi-scheduled units during dispatch intervals leading to frequency deviations. Its accident scale is: Unknown (two distinct spikes, involving 201 registered semi-scheduled units, but no quantified damage or outage magnitude reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 10-04-2026 and 11-04-2026. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all market participants including generators, retailers, and AEMO). Its cause type is: technical/operational (frequency instability due to semi-scheduled unit dispatch behavior). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Sequence of events across three dispatch intervals for multiple semi-scheduled units; 2. Inconsistent availability or output patterns of semi-scheduled units during the spikes; 3. Likely coordination or control issues among the 201 units.

title: Waratah BESS more significantly ramps, on Thursday 16th April 2026
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-11-07 long unplanned outage at Waratah BESS?
classified_content: The summary of the news "Waratah BESS more significantly ramps, on Thursday 16th April 2026" posted at 16-04-2026 01:50:00 PM is: The Waratah Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) experienced a significant ramp in output on Thursday 16th April 2026, following a long unplanned outage that began around November 2025. The second transformer, previously reported as operational for testing, came back online and triggered rapid output change alerts twice in quick succession, indicating increased activity after the extended period of reduced capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: on 16 April 2026, with ongoing potential effects from the prior outage and ramp-up. Its root_cause is: Long unplanned outage at Waratah BESS starting in late 2025, followed by partial restoration and testing of the second transformer. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 2025-11-07 (outage start) and 16-04-2026 (ramp event). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (Waratah BESS location). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely grid operators and electricity consumers in the region). Its cause type is: Technical failure (transformer/equipment issues). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Long unplanned outage of Waratah BESS; second transformer testing and rapid output changes on 16 April 2026.

title: A big clue to one causal factor in both frequency spikes (on Friday 10th April 2026 and Saturday 11th April 2026)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A big clue to one causal factor in both frequency spikes (on Friday 10th April 2026 and Saturday 11th April 2026)" posted at 14-04-2026 07:48:00 PM is: The article identifies a major causal factor behind two frequency spikes in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on April 10 and 11, 2026. Analysis of 86 wind farms and 115 solar farms reveals that the timing of these spikes aligns with a large number of semi-scheduled units switching from AEMO's AWEFS/ASEFS forecast systems back to SCADA, indicating this coordinated flipping event as the root cause. The author highlights concerns about system stability, control opacity, and potential implications for participants and costs.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: grid/system instability.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact during the frequency spikes on 10–11 April 2026, with ongoing concerns about system stability and control.; Its root_cause is: Simultaneous switching of many semi-scheduled wind and solar farms from AEMO’s AWEFS/ASEFS systems to SCADA, causing frequency deviations.; Its accident scale is: Unknown (frequency spikes affected the entire NEM but no specific scale given).; Its dates is: 10-11 April 2026 (event dates).; Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) – all five regions (Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, Tasmania).; Its affected key users is: NEM stakeholders, generators, system operators, and participants.; Its cause type is: Operational/systemic (semi-scheduled unit switching behaviour).; Its causes is: 1) Large number of wind farms (86) and solar farms (115) simultaneously flipping from AWEFS/ASEFS to SCADA; 2) This coincided with the timing of frequency spikes; 3) Potential issues with self-forecasting or semi-dispatch cap flag operations.

title: Across 201 x Semi-Scheduled units, which were the biggest over-performers (Saturday afternoon 11th April) driving the frequency high?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Across 201 x Semi-Scheduled units, which were the biggest over-performers (Saturday afternoon 11th April) driving the frequency high?" posted at 13-04-2026 03:12:00 PM is: The article describes a frequency spike on Saturday 11th April 2026 at 14:20 NEM time, caused by widespread over-performance of semi-scheduled wind and solar units across the National Electricity Market. Analysis of 201 units (115 solar farms, 86 wind farms) found 22 units with over-performance exceeding 25 MW (some over 50 MW), while only one unit had under-performance of that magnitude. This imbalance, contrary to the typical under-performance bias from semi-dispatch caps, raises system stability concerns and is attributed to a forecasting glitch and possible gaming by self-forecasters. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within the dispatch interval) and short-term (minutes to hours). Its root_cause is: Inaccurate UIGF forecasting and potential gaming by self-forecasters leading to over-generation relative to targets. Its accident scale is: 22 semi-scheduled units (10 solar, 12 wind) with over-performance >25 MW, some >50 MW, representing ~10% of all semi-scheduled units. Its dates is: 11-04-2026. Its affected region is: All regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: AEMO (system operator), market participants. Its cause type is: Operational/forecasting error. Its causes is: Glitch in UIGF forecasting, self-forecaster gaming, dispatch error.

title: A very similar spike in frequency (outside the NOFB) on Saturday 11th April 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A very similar spike in frequency (outside the NOFB) on Saturday 11th April 2026" posted at 13-04-2026 11:07:00 AM is: The article describes two similar frequency spikes in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on Friday 10th April (morning) and Saturday 11th April 2026 (afternoon), both caused by a glitch in AEMO’s UIGF forecasting system, possibly compounded by self-forecasters’ actions, leading to linear ramps peaking just outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band (50.176 Hz and 50.174 Hz). Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes during the dispatch intervals). Its root_cause is: Gremlins in the dispatch process related to UIGF forecasting systems at AEMO, with potential gaming by self-forecasters. Its accident scale is: Minor frequency excursions just outside the NOFB (50.176 Hz and 50.174 Hz), no reported system separation or load shedding. Its dates (if different from the main date) are: 10-04-2026 and 11-04-2026. Its affected region is: NEM Mainland (Australia). Its affected key users is: All grid participants and consumers (via frequency deviations). Its cause type is: Technical/operational (forecasting and dispatch system glitch). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Glitch in AEMO's UIGF forecasting; possible games by self-forecasters; dispatch process gremlins leading to linear ramp patterns.

title: Another case of Semi-Scheduled VRE over-production (this time at 14:20 on Saturday 11th April 2026)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The news article reports a semi-scheduled variable renewable energy (VRE) over-production event on Saturday 11 April 2026 at 14:20 NEM time, with a smaller secondary incident at 15:20. The main event involved an aggregate dispatch error of –1,193 MW, comprising –977 MW from wind farms and –316 MW from solar farms, all over-performing relative to their targets. This caused a frequency spike, mirroring a similar incident on Friday 10 April. The article notes that such collective over-performance is rare and notable for grid stability.

**Reasoning for attributes:**  
- **Relevance level:** Level 2 (Grid frequency issues) – The event directly caused a frequency spike, categorised under grid frequency issues.  
- **Category:** Grid frequency issues.  
- **Timeframe of impact:** Immediate – the over-production occurred within a single dispatch interval (14:20) and the frequency spike resolved shortly after.  
- **Root cause:** Aggregate over-performance of semi-scheduled VRE (wind and solar) relative to their dispatch targets, likely due to weather conditions causing higher-than-expected generation.  
- **Accident scale:** –1,193 MW total dispatch error.  
- **Dates:** 11-04-2026 (primary event), also referenced 10-04-2026 (similar incident).  
- **Affected region:** National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia.  
- **Affected key users:** Grid operators, market participants, and frequency control systems.  
- **Cause type:** Operational/technical (VRE over-generation).  
- **Causes:** (1) Over-performance of semi-scheduled wind farms (–977 MW), (2) Over-performance of semi-scheduled solar farms (–316 MW), (3) Resultant aggregate dispatch error causing frequency deviation.

Now, the final output in the exact format:

The summary of the news "Another case of Semi-Scheduled VRE over-production (this time at 14:20 on Saturday 11th April 2026)" posted at 12-04-2026 08:38:00 AM is: On Saturday 11 April 2026 at 14:20 NEM time, a rare collective over-performance of semi-scheduled wind and solar farms caused a –1,193 MW aggregate dispatch error (wind –977 MW, solar –316 MW) relative to targets, leading to a frequency spike. A smaller incident occurred at 15:20. This event is similar to a prior incident on Friday 10 April 2026. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Grid frequency issues). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (single dispatch interval). Its root_cause is: Aggregate over-performance of semi-scheduled VRE due to higher-than-expected generation. Its accident scale is: –1,193 MW dispatch error. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 11-04-2026 (primary), also 10-04-2026 (secondary). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Grid operators, market participants, frequency control systems. Its cause type is: Operational/technical. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Over-performance of semi-scheduled wind farms (–977 MW), (2) Over-performance of semi-scheduled solar farms (–316 MW), (3) Combined aggregate dispatch error causing frequency deviation.

title: Yes, it was a large over-performance from Wind Farms (many of them) that drove frequency above the NOFB on Friday 10th April 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Yes, it was a large over-performance from Wind Farms (many of them) that drove frequency above the NOFB on Friday 10th April 2026" posted at 11-04-2026 11:14:00 AM is: On 10 April 2026, a significant frequency spike above the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB) occurred in the National Electricity Market (NEM) due to a large collective over-performance of semi-scheduled wind farms during the 10:30 dispatch interval. Multiple wind farms, mostly in Victoria, generated substantially more power than their dispatch targets, with seven units over-performing by more than 50 MW and three additional units over 25 MW, while no unit under-performed significantly. This caused the frequency to rise to 50.174 Hz before recovering by 10:35. The Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) and coal fleet provided regulation FCAS to contain the frequency. The root cause remains under investigation, but the event highlights control and forecasting challenges with wind generation; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate, intraday (dispatch intervals 10:30 to 10:35 on 10 April 2026). Its root_cause is: large collective over-performance of semi-scheduled wind farms relative to dispatch targets, possibly due to sudden wind changes or control system errors. Its accident scale is: 10 wind farms over-performing by 25 MW or more, with 7 exceeding 50 MW; no equipment damage reported but a grid frequency excursion. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 10-04-2026. Its affected region is: primarily Victoria (9 units) and South Australia (1 unit) within the NEM. Its affected key users is: all electricity consumers in the NEM (grid frequency deviation). Its cause type is: operational/control issue (over-generation by semi-scheduled wind farms). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: wind farm over-performance due to either forecast error, control system malfunction, or unusual weather conditions; non-conformance notices issued to four units; lack of under-performance to balance the over-generation.

title: The second transformer at Waratah BESS is operational (for testing)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-11-07 long unplanned outage at Waratah BESS?
classified_content: The summary of the news "The second transformer at Waratah BESS is operational (for testing)" posted at 11-04-2026 10:03:00 AM is: The Waratah BESS second transformer (HVT2) has been re-energized for testing after being taken offline as a precaution, while the third transformer (HVT3) remains under repair following a failure caused by aggressive ramping in October 2025. The battery currently delivers 350MW under its System Integrity Protection Scheme (SIPS), with full capacity expected by the end of 2026. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term from November 2025 to end of 2026. Its root_cause is: Aggressive ramping of the battery caused transformer damage. Its accident scale is: Partial loss of 350MW capacity (out of 700MW). Its dates is: Original failure: 2025-11-07, testing: 2026-04-10, article date: 2026-04-11. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: National Electricity Market participants, System Integrity Protection Scheme. Its cause type is: Technical failure (transformer damage) and operational precaution. Its causes is: 1) Aggressive ramping in October 2025 led to HVT3 damage; 2) Precautionary shutdown of HVT2; 3) Ongoing remediation work.

title: Four big Non-Conformances at Wind Farms (coincidentally on Friday 10th April 2026 at the time of the frequency spike).  Some gremlin in the works?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Four big Non-Conformances at Wind Farms (coincidentally on Friday 10th April 2026 at the time of the frequency spike). Some gremlin in the works?" posted at 10-04-2026 is: On April 10, 2026, a frequency spike (reaching 50.174 Hz at 10:30) in the Australian National Electricity Market was closely followed by eight AEMO market notices declaring non-conformance for four Victorian wind farms—Elaine, Golden Plains East 1, Golden Plains East 2, and Ryan Corner—during the 10:35 and 10:40 dispatch intervals, with total underperformance of 689 MW (Elaine: 74 MW, GPWFEST1: 239 MW, GPWFEST2: 237 MW, RYANCWF1: 139 MW). The events suggest a direct temporal link between the grid disturbance and the wind farms' inability to meet dispatch targets. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (within the same day, from 10:30 to 10:40 on 10 April 2026). Its root_cause is: A frequency spike in the NEM that triggered underperformance or control issues at multiple semi-scheduled wind farms, leading to non-conformance. Its accident scale is: 4 wind farms, total non-conformance of 689 MW over a 5-minute interval. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 10-04-2026. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (within the NEM). Its affected key users is: AEMO, NEM market operators, and potentially electricity consumers (due to possible price impacts). Its cause type is: Technical / grid disturbance. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) A frequency spike occurred at 10:30 on 10 April 2026; 2) During the following dispatch intervals (10:35 and 10:40), four wind farms failed to conform to their dispatch targets, resulting in AEMO declaring non-conformance under clause 3.8.23 of the National Electricity Rules.

title: The curious case of a frequency spike (outside of the NOFB) on Friday 10th April 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The curious case of a frequency spike (outside of the NOFB) on Friday 10th April 2026" posted at 10-04-2026 03:30:00 PM is: A frequency spike to 50.174 Hz occurred in the NEM on 10 April 2026 at 10:30 NEM time, breaching the Normal Operating Frequency Band and triggering contingency FCAS responses; the spike ramped steadily up and down over two dispatch intervals, unlike typical events caused by sudden load trips or VRE issues, and its exact cause remains unknown, with speculation including a large load trip (e.g., data center), underperformance of variable renewable energy, or a data/operational glitch. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (minutes to hours). Its root_cause is: Unknown (speculated as possible large load trip, VRE issue, or data/operational glitch). Its accident scale is: single frequency spike (minor excursion). Its dates is: 10-04-2026. Its affected region is: NEM (Australia). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown (possibly operational or external). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: sudden trip of a large load (e.g., aluminium smelter or data center), underperformance of variable renewable energy fleet, data or operational glitch.

title: What’s up with Waratah BESS?  Is the second transformer back early?
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-11-07 long unplanned outage at Waratah BESS?
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s up with Waratah BESS?  Is the second transformer back early?" posted at 10-04-2026 02:40:00 PM is: The article examines recent changes in AEMO's MT PASA and ST PASA data for the Waratah BESS, which suggest the second transformer may be returning earlier than expected after a prolonged outage. A transformer failure on 18 October 2025 caused an unplanned outage initially expected until 3 May 2026, but updated availability data shows 350 MW becoming available from 12 April 2026, possibly due to transformer repair or adjustments in SIPS reservation. ST PASA data hints at a partial return to 700 MW capacity, though uncertainty remains and no official announcement from Akaysha Energy has been made. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 18 October 2025 (transformer failure) to at least 12 April 2026 (partial return), with full restoration timeline still uncertain. Its root_cause is: Transformer failure at Waratah BESS on 18 October 2025. Its accident scale is: One transformer failed, another proactively taken offline; total capacity loss of up to 700 MW for several months. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 18-10-2025 (transformer failure) and 10-04-2026 (news publication). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (within the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: AEMO, electricity market participants, and consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes is: Transformer failure on 18 October 2025 leading to unplanned outage; proactive outage of second transformer for in-situ testing and repair.

title: AEMO initiates Expedited Consultation to address ‘spammy’ automated MII notices
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO initiates Expedited Consultation to address ‘spammy’ automated MII notices" posted at 10-04-2026 11:36:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has launched an expedited consultation to tackle a surge in automated Market Notices for Manifestly Incorrect Inputs (MII), which stakeholders deem excessive and risk masking critical alerts. The spike stems from automated thresholds for FCAS (Frequency Control Ancillary Services) that activate when network outages raise islanding risks, prompting AEMO to seek industry feedback by 17 April 2026 to review the procedures.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from early 2026, with consultation period ending 17 April 2026; potential market notice clarity improvements thereafter. Its root_cause is: Automated FCAS threshold triggers under specific network outage conditions that increase islanding risk, leading to excessive MII notices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 10-04-2026. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: NEM stakeholders (market participants, AEMO, industry analysts). Its cause type is: Operational/procedural (automated threshold design). Its causes is: Increased network outages raising islanding risk; automated FCAS threshold procedures generating excessive MII market notices.

title: Daily count of MII market notices confirms sudden increase
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Daily count of MII market notices confirms sudden increase" posted at 10-04-2026 10:25:00 AM is: The article reports a sudden increase in daily counts of Manifestly Incorrect Input (MII) market notices, starting in March 2026, based on data since 2020. These notices typically indicate "prices subject to review" and are often accompanied by "prices unchanged" notices, suggesting a rise in data quality issues or market input errors during that period. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: March 2026 onwards (ongoing at time of publication). Its root_cause is: Unknown (the article does not specify the reason for the increase). Its accident scale is: Unknown (no quantitative scale provided). Its dates is: March 2026 (start of the increase). Its affected region is: Unknown (not explicitly stated, likely the electricity market area in which MII notices are used). Its affected key users is: Market operators and participants (generators, retailers) – implied but not specified. Its cause type is: Operational (data input errors triggering market notices). Its causes is: A summarized list: increase in manifestly incorrect input notices; potential underlying issues with data submission quality or market system changes, although the exact cause is not identified in the article.

title: The shift to long duration storage is inevitable – but current market design is not supporting investment at scale
author: Jamie Guthrie
classified_content: The summary of the news "The shift to long duration storage is inevitable – but current market design is not supporting investment at scale" posted at 09-04-2026 01:10:00 PM is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) requires long-duration storage to maintain reliability as coal plants retire, but current market design and financing models fail to support investment at scale due to inadequate revenue certainty, high merchant risk, and a lack of bankable contracting structures, creating a structural gap between system needs and funded assets. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with long-term implications for reliability and investment as coal exit accelerates. Its root_cause is: Lack of revenue certainty and bankable contracting structures for long-duration storage, misaligned market design that does not value system-wide benefits. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 09-04-2026. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) – Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, generators, storage developers, grid operator (AEMO), investors, and policymakers. Its cause type is: Market design and policy issue. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Accelerated coal-fired generation retirements (e.g., Liddell, Eraring, Yallourn); insufficient long-term revenue floor for long-duration storage; reliance on short-term price signals and FCAS revenues that are compressing; limited depth of contracted offtake for >8-hour products; high merchant exposure and lower capital efficiency compared to short-duration BESS; conventional project finance frameworks that disfavour long-lived assets.

title: ‘Running on Empty’ … a reminder of the implications of Shallow Storage
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "'Running on Empty' … a reminder of the implications of Shallow Storage" posted at 09-04-2026 08:24:00 AM is: The article examines how South Australia's battery fleet saw its aggregate state of charge plummet from near full (1,600 MWh) on 6 April 2026 to barely 1,000 MWh by 8 April 2026, after a ~24-hour period of poor wind and solar yield caused high prices. Batteries discharged into those high prices and were forced to recharge at elevated costs, highlighting the business and system risks of shallow storage and underscoring the need for deeper storage in the energy transition. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 6–9 April 2026. Its root_cause is: Low wind and solar yield (poor variable renewable energy generation) leading to high prices, forcing batteries to discharge and then recharge at elevated costs. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident; a market event). Its dates is: 6 April 2026 (start of low VRE period) to 9 April 2026 (article date). Its affected region is: South Australia (SA). Its affected key users is: Battery storage operators (large battery fleet) and indirectly electricity consumers via price impacts. Its cause type is: Natural phenomenon (low renewable resource) combined with market dynamics. Its causes is: a) Period of low wind overnight on 6–7 April; b) Day with lower solar yield on 7–8 April; c) Resulting high prices that triggered battery discharge and subsequent costly recharge.

title: ESIG releases report ‘Large Load Disturbance Events’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "ESIG releases report ‘Large Load Disturbance Events’" posted at 05-04-2026 05:20:00 PM is: The report, released on 25th March 2026, documents real-world grid disturbances caused by large loads such as data centers and AI training clusters, outlining two primary categories of events: ride-through failures where loads trip offline during voltage or frequency excursions, and load-driven oscillations from rapid power consumption changes or backup power signals, which can cascade into grid instability; the report recommends mandatory ride-through requirements, high-resolution metering, earlier coordination, and consistent data collection to ensure reliability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: ongoing (report highlights current and future risks); Its root_cause is: large load disturbances from ride-through failures and load-driven oscillations; Its accident scale is: documented but unspecified in magnitude (multiple real-world events); Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 25-03-2026 (report release date); Its affected region is: Unknown (likely U.S. or broad grid regions); Its affected key users is: system operators, utilities, regulators, and load developers; Its cause type is: technical/operational; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: ride-through failures of large loads during grid excursions, load-driven oscillations from rapid power consumption changes of AI clusters and signals from backup power equipment.

title: What happened at Macarthur Wind Farm (14 hours with a consistent Dispatch Error of approx 60MW)? … i.e. Part 6
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What happened at Macarthur Wind Farm (14 hours with a consistent Dispatch Error of approx 60MW)? … i.e. Part 6" posted at 02-04-2026 03:34:00 PM is: On April 2, 2026, Macarthur Wind Farm experienced a persistent Dispatch Error of approximately 60MW for 14 hours, beginning at 14:00 on April 1. The error arose when the UIGF jumped from 19MW to 81MW, but due to low wind, the target was unachievable under the Semi-Dispatch Cap, compounded by ROCUP ramp limits. This consistent error lined up exactly with the SDC period, raising questions about wind farm performance and grid stability. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Fuel supply issues (wind availability). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 14 hours from 14:00 April 1 to 04:00 April 2, 2026 (ongoing at time of report). Its root_cause is: Low wind speeds causing a mismatch between forecast UIGF and actual generation, leading to a persistent dispatch error under Semi-Dispatch Cap constraints. Its accident scale is: 60MW deviation for 14 hours. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-04-2026 to 02-04-2026. Its affected region is: Macarthur Wind Farm, Victoria, Australia (NEM). Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants, grid operators. Its cause type is: Operational/forecasting error. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Low wind speed; UIGF jump from 19MW to 81MW; ROCUP ramp limit of 74MW; unachievable Semi-Dispatch Cap; resulting in consistent Dispatch Error of ~60MW.

title: A quick look at Dispatch Error for each individual Wind Unit (at 03:05 on Thursday 2nd April 2026)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at Dispatch Error for each individual Wind Unit (at 03:05 on Thursday 2nd April 2026)" posted at 02-04-2026 03:32:00 PM is: This technical analysis examines the dispatch error for each semi-scheduled wind farm unit at 03:05 on 2 April 2026, following an earlier full-load trip of the Kogan Creek coal unit. The review of 84 wind units reveals a notable and persistent under-performance at the Macarthur Wind Farm, while most other units appear balanced. The context includes healthy regional prices and lower semi-dispatch cap incidence, which helped limit further frequency degradation after the initial trip. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather, turbine/generator failures, grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – the event and analysis are from the same day (2 April 2026), with impacts on frequency and dispatch balance occurring within minutes to hours. Its root_cause is: The full-load trip of the Kogan Creek coal unit (reported earlier) triggered a frequency drop, and the subsequent dispatch error analysis for wind units aimed to assess how wind farms contributed to or mitigated the grid response. Its accident scale is: One major coal unit trip (Kogan Creek) and one wind farm with significant under-performance (Macarthur Wind Farm); the remaining wind units were within normal dispatch error. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 02-04-2026 (same date as the post and the event). Its affected region is: Likely the National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, given the reference to Kogan Creek and Macarthur Wind Farm. Its affected key users is: Grid operators (AEMO), wind farm operators, and balancing market participants. Its cause type is: Generator failure (coal unit trip) and operational deviation (wind farm under-dispatch). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Full-load trip of Kogan Creek coal unit at approximately 03:05 on 2 April 2026; [2] Under-performance of Macarthur Wind Farm (large and continuous dispatch error) during the same period; [3] Overall healthy regional prices and low semi-dispatch cap incidence limited frequency deterioration.

Most other coal units supported the grid with negative Dispatch Errors (up to ~-20 MW), but a few units still showed positive errors, a curiosity noted for further exploration. title: What helped (and what didn’t help) to contain the frequency drop with this morning’s Kogan trip?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What helped (and what didn’t help) to contain the frequency drop with this morning’s Kogan trip?" posted at 02-04-2026 01:11:00 PM is: A full-load trip of the Kogan Creek coal unit (KPP_1) at the 03:05 Dispatch Interval on Thursday 2nd April 2026 caused a +720MW underperformance, dragging down grid frequency. Analysis of dispatch errors by fuel type shows that large battery energy storage systems (BESS) contributed the most to arresting the drop (-375MW), followed by other coal units (-246MW), hydro (-51MW), and gas (-22MW), while wind had a harmful positive error (+44MW). The event was contained within subsequent intervals, but the article notes that frequency was also low in the next interval and that the trip occurred during night-time, which likely mitigated worse outcomes. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (occurred at 03:05 Dispatch Interval on 02-04-2026, with effects lasting at least through the 03:10 interval). Its root_cause is: Full-load trip of Kogan Creek unit KPP_1. Its accident scale is: Single unit trip (720MW underperformance). Its dates is: 02-04-2026. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market, Australia). Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants. Its cause type is: Generation failure (turbine/generator). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Trip of Kogan Creek unit KPP_1.

title: Is the battery rollout following in solar’s footsteps — and is a battery correlation penalty looming?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Is the battery rollout following in solar’s footsteps — and is a battery correlation penalty looming?" posted at 02-04-2026 01:00:00 PM is: The article draws parallels between the historical solar boom and the current battery storage buildout in Australia’s National Electricity Market, warning that a similar 'battery correlation penalty' may emerge as the majority of batteries (utility-scale and behind-the-meter) share short durations (1–2 hours) and diurnal cycling patterns, leading to market saturation. Evidence includes declining FCAS revenues since late 2023, compressed intraday two-hour price spreads (particularly in Queensland during the summer of 2025–2026), and an asymmetry where grid-scale batteries face full wholesale price exposure while behind-the-meter systems remain shielded. About 7.5 GW of utility-scale batteries are operating or in commissioning, with another 7.7 GW expected within 24–36 months, and 4–5 GW of residential capacity installed in nine months via a federal subsidy scheme, accelerating faster than solar ever did. The article notes that government underwriting (CIS, LTESA, VRET schemes) now transfers a portion of long-term revenue risk to public balance sheets, potentially delaying market signals for overcapacity.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).; Its category is: market reactions.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing (from late 2023 to present) and future (next 24–36 months, with projected acceleration).; Its root_cause is: Highly correlated operating patterns of battery storage (similar durations and diurnal cycling) leading to market saturation and erosion of revenue, analogous to the solar correlation penalty from overdeployment of solar in the NEM.; Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident; the scale of battery rollout is 7.5 GW utility-scale operating, 7.7 GW additional, and 4–5 GW residential in nine months).; Its dates are: Article date 02-04-2026; observed impacts from late 2023 to summer 2025–2026.; Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, with specific mentions of Queensland, Victoria, and eastern Australia.; Its affected key users are: Utility-scale battery developers and investors, behind-the-meter residential battery owners, policymakers, and electricity consumers indirectly affected by price spread compression.; Its cause type is: Market structural issue / investment risk / policy-induced asymmetry.; Its causes are: 1. Rapid deployment of batteries with similar durations (1–2 hours) and diurnal cycling patterns; 2. Saturation in FCAS markets from late 2023; 3. Decreasing intraday two-hour price spreads, especially in Queensland during summer 2025–2026; 4. Asymmetry between grid-scale BESS (exposed to wholesale prices) and behind-the-meter systems (shielded); 5. Government underwriting (CIS, LTESA, VRET schemes) transferring long-term revenue risk to public balance sheets, potentially delaying corrective market signals.

title: A first look at the frequency performance, with today’s trip at Kogan Creek
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A first look at the frequency performance, with today’s trip at Kogan Creek" posted at 02-04-2026 10:27:00 AM is: A full-load trip of Kogan Creek unit KPP_1 occurred between 02:55 and 03:00 NEM time on 2 April 2026, dropping from 722 MW to 0 MW. The resultant frequency fell to 49.88 Hz (still within the Normal Operating Frequency Band) and took about 10 minutes to return to the PFR Deadband, aided by inertia, Primary Frequency Response, and Regulation FCAS. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Grid users in NEM (frequency deviation within normal operating band, no contingency FCAS triggered). Its cause type is: Forced outage (generator trip). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Trip of Kogan Creek unit KPP_1 from 722MW to 0MW at approximately 02:59:54 NEM time on 2 April 2026.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "First glimpses of output at REECE2 unit, following (possible) fire in August 2025" posted at 01-04-2026 09:27:00 AM is: The REECE2 unit at Reece Power Station, which had been out of service since a possible fire on 5 August 2025, showed its first output on 1 April 2026, ending almost seven months of outage. title: What impact might Fuel Rationing have on the National Electricity Market?

classified_content: The summary of the news "What impact might Fuel Rationing have on the National Electricity Market?" posted at 29-03-2026 02:46:00 PM is: The article examines how fuel rationing, already occurring in some Australian locations due to the Middle East war, could threaten electricity market security. title: Delving deeper into coal unit (re) starts over >17 years … January 2010 to March 2026
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Long term trend of coal unit performance
classified_content: The summary of the news "Delving deeper into coal unit (re) starts over >17 years … January 2010 to March 2026" posted at 29-03-2026 01:16:00 PM is: This article examines long-term trends in coal unit performance in the National Electricity Market (NEM) from January 2010 to March 2026, focusing on the classification and frequency of unit starts (hot restarts, hot starts, warm starts, cold starts) across 44 still-operational coal units. It confirms two key findings: coal units are not tripping or restarting more often overall, but when they do go offline, they tend to be offline for longer periods. The analysis shows a shift away from warmer starts and hot restarts toward more cold starts, indicating extended outages that reduce the dependability of coal-fired generation and have implications for electricity market supply and pricing. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term trend from January 2010 to March 2026, with ongoing impact. Its root_cause is: Aging coal units and changing operational patterns (e.g., increased two-shifting and longer maintenance outages) leading to more cold starts and longer offline durations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): 29-03-2026. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers in the NEM). Its cause type is: Operational trend. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Increased cold starts; decreased hot restarts and warm starts; longer offline durations per start; shift from warmer to colder start types over the 16+ year period.

title: A quick initial look at operations at the Wambo Wind Farm (WAMBOWF1)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick initial look at operations at the Wambo Wind Farm (WAMBOWF1)" posted at 26-03-2026 11:44:00 AM is: The article analyzes operational data for the Wambo Wind Farm (WAMBOWF1), a 50:50 joint venture between Stanwell Corporation and Cubico Sustainable Investments, focusing on its commissioning phase through 2025 and into early 2026. It notes that the unit only achieved part-load by end-2025, experienced a prolonged outage in October 2026 (presumably commissioning-related), and exhibited significant dispatch errors—particularly large negative errors (over-performance) in March 2026. There are also hints of high-temperature de-rating during hotter periods in 2025. The findings highlight the complexities of commissioning and the potential implications for system frequency and frequency performance payments.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 2025 through March 2026 and beyond, as commissioning continues. Its root_cause is: Commissioning process of the WAMBOWF1 unit, including hold points, partial load operation, and associated dispatch errors. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 2025-2026 (event dates span the reporting period, with specific instances in March 2026 and October 2026). Its affected region is: Wambo Wind Farm (likely in Queensland, Australia, within the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Stanwell Corporation, Cubico Sustainable Investments, and NEM market participants (including system operators and other generators). Its cause type is: Technical/operational. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Commissioning hold points leading to part-load operation; prolonged outage in October 2026; possible high-temperature de-rating; large dispatch errors (both positive and negative) related to semi-scheduled unit obligations.

title: A quick introduction to the gyrations on the ‘N>>NIL_060_051’ Constraint Equation during NSW Market Suspension

classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick introduction to the gyrations on the ‘N>>NIL_060_051’ Constraint Equation during NSW Market Suspension" posted at 24-03-2026 04:12:00 PM is: The article discusses the gyrations in the constraint equation N>>NIL_060_051 during the NSW market suspension on 23 March 2026, highlighting a drop in the RHS from 1,246.04 MW to 1,119.59 MW at 12:45, which led to a semi-regular cycle with a marginal value of -$1,026.38/MWh, constraining down 7 units and leaving 5 units out of the money; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on 23 March 2026 during the market suspension and constraint gyrations; Its root_cause is: The gyrations were caused by a drop in the RHS of the constraint equation, triggering feedback loops that led to a semi-regular cycle; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 23-03-2026; Its affected region is: NSW (New South Wales); Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: technical/operational; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: ["Drop in RHS from 1246.04 MW to 1119.59 MW", "Feedback loops from constraint binding", "Semi-regular cycle established"].

title: Managing bids during the market suspension on 23 March 2026
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Managing bids during the market suspension on 23 March 2026" posted at 24-03-2026 02:34:00 PM is: On 23 March 2026, the New South Wales (NSW) region experienced a market suspension from 11:45 to 18:30, during which suspension pricing was applied, with spot prices ranging from 44 to 100 $/MWh. AEMO continued bidding and dispatch where practical, but the automatic generation control system was inoperable; nonetheless, bids were still processed. An analysis of rebids by fuel type revealed varied behaviors: some units (e.g., thermal, hydro, gas) showed little change in rebidding patterns, while battery storage units typically halted or altered rebid frequency soon after the suspension start and resumed near its end. The article examines how participants managed their offers under suspension pricing rules.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 23 March 2026, from 11:45 to 18:30. Its root_cause is: Unknown (market suspension due to unspecified system instability, but attributed to grid/system instability). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 23-03-2026. Its affected region is: NSW region. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants (generators, especially thermal, hydro, gas, and battery storage units). Its cause type is: Market suspension. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMO declared market suspension for NSW region; automatic generation control system was not operable; suspension pricing was applied; bidding and dispatch continued under special rules.

title: Initial energy storage involved in issues
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Initial energy storage involved in issues" posted at 24-03-2026 09:23:00 AM is: With the benefit of next-day data, it appears that initial energy storage data was involved in the SCADA issues of March 23, 2026. An early prototype estimating storage levels for BESS units captured missing data from NSW batteries, revealing interruptions in storage level signals to AEMO. Several units including Broken Hill Battery (BHB1), Capital (CAPBES1), Waratah (WTAHB1), and Quorn Park Hybrid experienced data glitches such as missing actuals, sharp changes, or drops to zero. The article highlights these operational glitches and references market suspension. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (day of event, March 23, 2026). Its root_cause is: SCADA system data transmission failure or software glitch. Its accident scale is: Unknown, but multiple BESS units affected. Its dates are: 23-03-2026 (event date), 24-03-2026 (post date). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (NSW batteries). Its affected key users is: AEMO (Australian Energy Market Operator) and BESS operators. Its cause type is: Operational glitch / SCADA system failure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Interrupted initial energy storage level signals to AEMO; missing data for multiple BESS units; sharp drops to zero for some units.

title: What’s up with Waratah BESS … again?!
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-11-07 long unplanned outage at Waratah BESS?
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s up with Waratah BESS … again?!" posted at 24-03-2026 08:17:00 AM is: On Monday 23 March 2026, the Waratah BESS (WTAHB1) experienced a rapid drop in stored energy from 794 MWh to 0 MWh within minutes, accompanied by a plant-related rebid with reason 'Auxiliary Load – See Log' and reduced market offers. This occurred amid reported SCADA issues that temporarily affected the NSW market, raising questions about the unit's ability to provide System Integrity Protection Scheme (SIPS) services. No official update from operator Akaysha Energy was available as of Tuesday morning 24 March 2026. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, still ongoing as of 24 March 2026. Its root_cause is: Auxiliary load issue or SCADA-related data pollution leading to rapid discharge and rebid. Its accident scale is: Unknown (involves a single unit across three transformers, but only one in service). Its dates are: 23-03-2026 (event date), 24-03-2026 (news date). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants, SIPS operators. Its cause type is: Technical failure (auxiliary load/SCADA). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Stored energy dropping from 794 MWh to 0 MWh between 08:00 and 08:10 NEM time 23 March 2026; 2) Plant-related rebid received at 07:04 with reason 'Auxiliary Load – See Log'; 3) Reduced volume offered to market since ~05:00; 4) AEMO reported SCADA issues earlier that day potentially affecting energy storage data.

title: We’ve moved from ‘Just in Case’ to be ‘Just in Time’ … and its hurting us, with the war in the Middle East (but where else is this a consideration?)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2026 Energy Security Concerns with the War in the Middle East
classified_content: The summary of the news "We’ve moved from ‘Just in Case’ to be ‘Just in Time’ … and its hurting us, with the war in the Middle East (but where else is this a consideration?)" posted at 23-03-2026 02:43:00 PM is: This article analyzes Australia's vulnerability to oil supply disruptions from the Middle East war, highlighting the shift from a 'just in case' to a 'just in time' supply chain. It cites experts warning of potential fuel rationing due to panic buying, Australia's 90% fuel import dependency, and lack of domestic refineries. The piece draws parallels to electricity grid resilience, arguing that insufficient shaping and firming capacity (beyond short-duration storage) makes the power system similarly exposed. It calls for dual action: increasing fuel stocks and accelerating electrification, while also strengthening grid reliability to avoid future crises.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with potential medium-term impacts if the Middle East war persists. Its root_cause is: Middle East geopolitical conflict causing global oil supply constraints, combined with Australia's heavy import reliance, lack of strategic fuel reserves, and a 'just in time' supply chain that amplifies panic buying behavior. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 23-03-2026. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: General fuel consumers and, by extension, electricity grid operators and end-users. Its cause type is: Geopolitical and systemic planning failure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Middle East war causing global oil supply uncertainty; 2) Australia's 90% fuel import dependency; 3) Only two domestic oil refineries remaining; 4) 'Just in time' supply chain without adequate strategic reserves; 5) Panic buying by consumers leading to 35-40% demand surge; 6) Insufficient investment in grid shaping and firming capacity for future resilience.

title: Final Report released ‘Grid Incident in Spain and Portugal on 28 April 2025’

classified_content: The summary of the news "Final Report released ‘Grid Incident in Spain and Portugal on 28 April 2025’" posted at 21-03-2026 01:23:00 PM is: The final report on the 28 April 2025 blackout in Spain and Portugal, released by ENTSO-E on 20 March 2026, identifies root causes including cascading generator disconnections, inadequate voltage control, and oscillations mitigation failures, while emphasizing the need for higher resilience in grids with increasing renewable penetration; the report also notes missing data hindered full analysis; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 28 April 2025; Its root_cause is: cascading disconnections of generation, voltage control and oscillations mitigation failures; Its accident scale is: large-scale blackout affecting the Iberian Peninsula; Its dates (if different) are: event: 28 April 2025, report release: 20 March 2026; Its affected region is: Spain and Portugal (Iberian Peninsula); Its affected key users is: all electricity consumers; Its cause type is: technical; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: cascading generator trips, insufficient voltage control, oscillations, missing data for some generation trips.

title: To what extent did high-temperature de-rating contribute to large under-performance across Semi-Scheduled Wind fleet on Tuesday 27th January 2026?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "To what extent did high-temperature de-rating contribute to large under-performance across Semi-Scheduled Wind fleet on Tuesday 27th January 2026?" posted at 12-03-2026 04:17:00 PM is: The article analyzes the aggregate under-performance (positive dispatch error) of semi-scheduled wind farms on 27 January 2026, attributing it primarily to high-temperature de-rating. It shows that South Australia and Victoria were the main drivers, with a peak NEM-wide error of +673 MW at 15:15. Correlation between air temperature spikes and under-performance indicates heat-induced de-rating, exacerbated by a cool change. This created sustained frequency drag that required intervention from scheduled units (BESS, coal).; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: Extreme weather (high-temperature de-rating) and grid/system instability (frequency drag).; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 27 January 2026 (the day of the event).; Its root_cause is: High ambient temperatures causing de-rating of wind turbine output, combined with a rapid cool change affecting wind conditions.; Its accident scale is: Large under-performance across the semi-scheduled wind fleet, with an aggregate dispatch error peaking at +673 MW NEM-wide, prolonged over several hours in Victoria and South Australia.; Its dates is: 27 January 2026.; Its affected region is: Primarily South Australia and Victoria, with lesser impact in NSW, Queensland, and Tasmania.; Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled wind farms (the under-performing units), system operators (managing frequency), and scheduled units (BESS and coal) providing frequency control services.; Its cause type is: Environmental (extreme heat) leading to technical de-rating.; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: High-temperature de-rating of wind turbines in South Australia and Victoria due to record heat; rapid cool change passing from west to east reducing temperatures but also causing wind fluctuations; correlation between air temperature trends and aggregate dispatch error confirms heat as a significant factor.

title: Delving deeper, into periods of under-performance across Semi-Scheduled Solar Farms on Tuesday 27th January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Delving deeper, into periods of under-performance across Semi-Scheduled Solar Farms on Tuesday 27th January 2026" posted at 12-03-2026 02:31:00 PM is: This article analyzes the collective under-performance (positive Dispatch Error) of semi-scheduled solar farms across the National Electricity Market (NEM) on 27 January 2026, breaking down the data by region and unit-level Semi-Dispatch Cap (SDC) status. It finds that the morning's large, contiguous under-performance was almost entirely driven by NSW solar farms operating under SDC restrictions, where the one-sided 'follow dispatch target' rule caused systematic deviations. The afternoon/evening period showed smaller, less contiguous errors with contributions from QLD and VIC, possibly due to forecasting challenges with waning solar irradiance. The analysis questions the scalability and sustainability of the semi-scheduled category under current arrangements. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 27 January 2026. Its root_cause is: Semi-Dispatch Cap (SDC) restrictions imposing a one-way dimensionality on semi-scheduled units, leading to systematic under-performance when subject to dispatch targets. Its accident scale is: Unknown (systemic performance issue, not an accident). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 27-01-2026. Its affected region is: NEM (primarily NSW, with contributions from QLD and VIC). Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled solar farms. Its cause type is: Regulatory/operational (Semi-Dispatch Cap design). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Semi-Dispatch Cap flag being ON for many units, forcing them to follow dispatch targets; (2) The one-sided nature of 'follow dispatch target' for semi-scheduled units (can only under-perform relative to available resource); (3) Possibly increased forecasting difficulty during the afternoon period with waning sun.

title: Three time-ranges of interest, with respect to aggregate ‘Dispatch Error’ by Fuel Type on Tuesday 27th January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Three time-ranges of interest, with respect to aggregate ‘Dispatch Error’ by Fuel Type on Tuesday 27th January 2026" posted at 11-03-2026 10:48:00 AM is: This article analyzes dispatch errors by fuel type on 27 January 2026, a day marked by extreme heat in the south followed by a pronounced cool change, which caused semi-scheduled wind and solar units to frequently over-perform relative to targets, forcing fully scheduled units to work hard using PFR and Regulation FCAS to maintain frequency stability, and three time-ranges of interest are highlighted for further evaluation. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 27 January 2026. Its root_cause is: Extreme heat in the south followed by a cool change causing over-performance of semi-scheduled renewable generation and grid frequency instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 27 January 2026. Its affected region is: Southern region of the National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: All electricity market participants in the affected region. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes is: 1) Extreme heat in the south; 2) Pronounced cool change; 3) Over-performance of semi-scheduled wind and solar relative to targets; 4) Increased reliance on frequency control ancillary services to maintain grid stability.

title: A quick initial look at Dispatch Error by Fuel Type, and System Frequency, with the AEMO operations glitch on Tuesday 10th March 2026

Its affected key users is: Large Solar Farms (over-performing) and scheduled units (BESS, Coal, Hydro) providing frequency control. Its cause type is: Operational/system glitch. title: Intraday price spreads thinned out in most regions over summer 2025–26
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Intraday price spreads thinned out in most regions over summer 2025–26" posted at 06-03-2026 03:55:00 PM is: The article reviews price outcomes for the Australian NEM over summer 2025-26, finding that intraday 2-hour price spreads were significantly lower and less volatile compared to recent summers, driven by higher wind production from new farms, milder (though above-average) temperatures, stable coal availability, and a sharp increase in both utility-scale and behind-the-meter battery storage; notable exceptions include South Australia, which experienced extreme volatility on 26 January due to high temperatures and depleted battery reserves, but overall the summer was relatively quiet. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2025-26 (December 2025 to February 2026). Its root_cause is: A combination of favorable seasonal conditions—higher wind generation, milder heat, stable coal supply, and growing battery storage—that reduced intraday price volatility across most NEM regions. Its accident scale is: N/A (no accident; routine market conditions). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 06-03-2026. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) – specifically Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, and Tasmania. Its affected key users is: Wholesale electricity market participants, including generators, retailers, and traders. Its cause type is: Market conditions (combination of supply-side factors and weather). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Higher wind production due to new wind farms (Golden Plains, MacIntyre, Clarke Creek); 2) Above-average but milder temperatures compared to record heat of previous summers; 3) Coal availability broadly stable (no sharp decline in NSW/QLD, gradual decline in VIC/NSW); 4) Significant growth in utility-scale battery storage capacity; 5) Continued increase in behind-the-meter home storage.

title: How much volume has been traded on the ASX’s new morning & evening peak futures so far?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "How much volume has been traded on the ASX’s new morning & evening peak futures so far?" posted at 06-03-2026 08:00:00 AM is: The article reports that since their launch in mid-2025, ASX Energy's new Morning Peak and Evening Peak electricity futures have traded negligible volumes—only 58 Evening Peak contracts and zero Morning Peak contracts across NSW, QLD, VIC, and SA as of March 2026, compared to tens of thousands for traditional baseload futures. The lack of liquidity highlights the challenge of contract innovation in the NEM, with ASX Energy negotiating with market makers to boost trading. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Since June 2025, ongoing but currently minimal impact on electricity prices. Its root_cause is: Low market participant adoption and lack of market makers. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: June 30, 2025 to March 5, 2026. Its affected region is: NSW, QLD, VIC, SA (NEM regions). Its affected key users is: Electricity retailers, generators, traders (hedgers). Its cause type is: Market development / product innovation. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Launch of Morning Peak and Evening Peak futures; low trading volumes; negotiations with market makers.

title: War in the Middle East already (appears to be) flowing through to Q2 contract prices
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2026 Energy Security Concerns with the War in the Middle East
classified_content: The summary of the news "War in the Middle East already (appears to be) flowing through to Q2 contract prices" posted at 05-03-2026 03:06:00 PM is: The article discusses how the outbreak of war in the Middle East on 28 February 2026, starting with Israeli attacks that killed Iran’s Ayatollah, has rapidly impacted Australian electricity futures markets. Within days, Qatar Energy halted LNG production on 2 March and declared force majeure, leading to noticeable upticks in Q2 2026 base and cap contract prices across all mainland NEM regions (QLD, NSW, VIC, SA), while Q1 prices softened. Traders are reacting to fuel supply fears reminiscent of the 2022 energy crisis. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal, gas) – specifically LNG/gas supply. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – effects observed in futures trading from 2 to 5 March 2026 for Q2 2026 contracts. Its root_cause is: Geopolitical conflict in the Middle East (Israel’s attacks on Iran) causing Qatar Energy to stop LNG production and declare force majeure. Its accident scale is: Unknown (this is a geopolitical conflict and supply disruption, not an accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 28 February 2026 (attack); 2 March 2026 (Qatar LNG production halt). Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia (Australian National Electricity Market regions). Its affected key users is: Electricity traders, generators, and consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Geopolitical conflict and fuel supply disruption. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Israel’s ‘Operation Epic Fury’/‘Operation Roaring Lion’ attacks on Iran killing Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on 28 February 2026; 2) Qatar Energy stopping LNG production on 2 March 2026; 3) Qatar Energy declaring force majeure on 5 March 2026.

title: Qatar Energy declares Force Majeure
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2026 Energy Security Concerns with the War in the Middle East
classified_content: The summary of the news "Qatar Energy declares Force Majeure" posted at 05-03-2026 06:52:00 AM is: On 4th March 2026, Qatar Energy declared Force Majeure on LNG production following a halt announced on 2nd March, amid escalating Middle East war concerns, threatening global gas supplies and potentially impacting electricity markets reliant on LNG fuel.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal, gas); Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate and ongoing from 2nd March 2026; Its root_cause is: Middle East war and security concerns leading to production disruption; Its accident scale is: Force Majeure declaration covering LNG production stoppage; Its dates are: 04-03-2026 (Force Majeure declaration) – note news date is 05-03-2026; Its affected region is: global, particularly LNG-importing regions such as Asia and Europe; Its affected key users are: electricity generators, gas-fired power plants, and LNG buyers; Its cause type is: geopolitical/operational; Its causes are: middle East war leading to security concerns; decision to halt LNG production on 2nd March; declaration of Force Majeure on 4th March.
