title: Qatar Energy to stop production of LNG (on 2nd March 2026)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2026 Energy Security Concerns with the War in the Middle East
classified_content: The summary of the news "Qatar Energy to stop production of LNG (on 2nd March 2026)" posted at 03-03-2026 is: Qatar Energy, the world's largest LNG exporter, announced it will halt LNG production starting 2 March 2026, triggered by the bombing in Iran and subsequent retaliation, raising memories of the 2022 international gas market disruptions that cascaded into Australia's domestic energy crisis and highlighting increasing risks for the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: fuel supply issues (gas). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate from 2 March 2026, with duration dependent on how long the Middle East situation persists and unfolds. Its root_cause is: Bombing in Iran, resulting retaliation, and the decision by Qatar Energy to stop LNG production. Its accident scale is: Global – Qatar is the largest LNG exporter, so the scale is massive, affecting international gas supply chains. Its dates is: 2 March 2026 (event date; news posted 3 March 2026). Its affected region is: Global, particularly countries reliant on Qatari LNG, including Australia (NEM) and other Asian/European markets. Its affected key users is: Electricity generators using natural gas, industrial consumers, residential customers, and LNG importers. Its cause type is: Geopolitical conflict and deliberate production halt. Its causes is: Bombing in Iran, subsequent retaliation, Qatar Energy's decision to stop LNG production.

title: Basslink gets final revenue determination for operation as a regulated service
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Basslink gets final revenue determination for operation as a regulated service" posted at 03-03-2026 08:51:00 AM is: On 27 February 2026, the AER published its final decision on Basslink's revenue for the 2026–30 period, allowing $459.5 million (slightly less than Basslink's revised proposal) to be recovered from customers, with 75% from Victorian and 25% from Tasmanian customers, leading to small bill increases; the decision also notes negotiations for Frequency Control Special Protection Scheme (FCSPS) services and that APA will decide whether to convert Basslink from a market network service to a prescribed transmission service from July 2026; Its relevance level is: Level 2; Its category is: grid frequency issues; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2026–2030; Its root_cause is: AER final decision on Basslink revenue determination; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: 27 February 2026 (decision), 2026-30 period; Its affected region is: Victoria and Tasmania; Its affected key users is: Victorian and Tasmanian residential and small business customers; Its cause type is: Regulatory/economic; Its causes are: AER final decision, Basslink's conversion application, end of previous agreement with TasHydro, FCSPS negotiations.

title: AFR (and elsewhere) notes more machinations at Waratah BESS
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-11-07 long unplanned outage at Waratah BESS?
classified_content: The summary of the news "AFR (and elsewhere) notes more machinations at Waratah BESS" posted at 27-02-2026 03:42:00 PM is: The article reports ongoing delays in returning the Waratah Super Battery to full 850MW capacity due to transformer failures. After a transformer (HVT3) failed in October 2025, a replacement is scheduled for Q3 2026, while a second transformer (HVT2) was also taken offline for testing. Current market-available capacity has dropped from 350MW to 150MW as of November 2026 due to SIPS transition rules, with full service not expected until late 2026. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: October 2025 to late 2026 (ongoing). Its root_cause is: Failure of transformer HVT3 at the Waratah BESS. Its accident scale is: One transformer failed (HVT3), another taken offline for remediation (HVT2). Its dates (if different from main date) is: Original failure in October 2025; replacement expected Q3 2026. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) grid, Australia. Its affected key users is: Grid operator (AEMO), participants in the National Electricity Market, Akaysha Energy. Its cause type is: Equipment failure (transformer). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: HVT3 transformer failure in October 2025; subsequent precautionary testing and remediation of HVT2 transformer.

title: Market Price Cap to increase to $23,200/MWh from 1st July 2026
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Market Price Cap to increase to $23,200/MWh from 1st July 2026" posted at 27-02-2026 03:36:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) published its annual update to the NEM’s reliability settings, confirming the Market Price Cap (MPC) will rise to $23,200/MWh for the 2026–27 financial year, effective from July 1, 2026. This is the second step in a multi-year staged ‘rebuild’ of the cap following a prior increase to $20,300/MWh in FY 2025–26, with a final increase planned for 2027–28. The Cumulative Price Threshold (CPT) will also rise to $2,225,900/MWh from the same date. The increases are larger than typical inflation‑linked adjustments because they are part of a pathway set under the AEMC’s 2023 rule change to raise the base rate of the MPC and CPT.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From July 1, 2026 onward (future implementation). Its root_cause is: AEMC’s 2023 rule change to increase the base rate of the MPC and CPT as part of a multi‑year step‑up pathway. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different) is: 01-07-2026 (effective date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Generators, retailers, and end‑use consumers. Its cause type is: Regulatory/Policy change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMC’s annual update to NEM reliability settings; prior step‑up on 1 July 2025; staged rebuild under 2023 rule change.

title: How realistic are those wind capacity factor assumptions in the draft 2026 ISP?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "How realistic are those wind capacity factor assumptions in the draft 2026 ISP?" posted at 26-02-2026 06:30:00 PM is: This article examines the AEMO's draft 2026 Integrated System Plan (ISP), which assumes materially higher wind capacity factors (over 40% for several Renewable Energy Zones) compared to the 2024 ISP, based on stakeholder feedback and a methodology revision incorporating higher hub heights and granular data. Using 2025 real-world data from the Generator Statistical Digest, it shows that median availability factor of operating wind farms is 35.35% and only 8 out of 84 units exceeded 40%, questioning the realism of the assumptions amid growing curtailment and no clear efficiency gains. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (planning horizon up to 2026 and beyond). Its root_cause is: Stakeholder feedback and AEMO's methodology revision to use higher hub heights and granular wind data. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates is: 26-02-2026 06:30:00 PM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (Australia), various Renewable Energy Zones (REZs). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, policymakers, wind farm developers. Its cause type is: Planning assumption update. Its causes is: Stakeholder submissions advocating for higher capacity factors; AEMO review of wind trace data; adoption of higher hub heights and granular data source.

title: The state-of-charge for 2025: The NEM’s battery fleet crosses the chasm
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "The state-of-charge for 2025: The NEM’s battery fleet crosses the chasm" posted at 24-02-2026 06:30:00 AM is: The article analyzes the rapid growth and evolving market dynamics of the National Electricity Market's (NEM) battery fleet as of early 2026, highlighting that 7.5 GW / 16.6 GWh of utility-scale battery capacity is now operational, with 19 new units added in 2025 and an additional 7.7 GW under construction. Monthly revenue has become more volatile, driven largely by energy arbitrage, though FCAS revenues spiked in mid-2025 in regions like South Australia due to local procurement constraints. Batteries are increasingly involved in price setting, but that influence is spread across more operators, intensifying competition. Network curtailment emerged as a new factor, with an estimated 90,929 MWh curtailed in 2025, and the Frequency Performance Payments scheme provided a net $2.14m in revenue for the fleet. The article notes that behind-the-meter battery storage surged, with 219,000 home systems installed since July 2025, adding 4.7 GWh of capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 2025 onward. Its root_cause is: Rapid deployment of utility-scale and behind-the-meter battery storage, combined with changing market dynamics and policy support. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 2025. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market, Australia). Its affected key users is: Battery operators, electricity market participants, consumers. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and technological adoption. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Rapid build-out of utility-scale batteries; 2) Surge in behind-the-meter home battery installations; 3) Localized FCAS procurement due to interconnector outages; 4) Network constraints leading to curtailment of battery output; 5) Introduction of Frequency Performance Payments mechanism.

title: New FCAS resources dominate enabled levels in 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "New FCAS resources dominate enabled levels in 2025" posted at 23-02-2026 12:11:00 PM is: The article, based on GSD2025 data, reveals that inverter-based resources—predominantly grid-scale batteries, with smaller contributions from wind, solar, and virtual power plants (VPPs)—have become the dominant providers of all Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) in 2025, continuing a trend observed since 2023. Traditional units like Yallourn Power Station have exited FCAS bidding, and the majority of new enablement comes from batteries, though ancillary service units (often smaller batteries) also hold a significant share, especially in Raise markets. The trend is expected to persist as battery storage deployment increases, further offsetting traditional generation. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from 2023 to 2025. Its root_cause is: Transition from traditional synchronous generators to inverter-based resources (batteries, VPPs) for FCAS provision. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: Unknown. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market dynamics / technology shift. Its causes is: Increasing battery deployment; retirement of traditional generators from FCAS; market incentives for new resources.

title: Yallourn Power Station no longer bidding FCAS services (as seen via GSD2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Yallourn Power Station no longer bidding FCAS services (as seen via GSD2025)" posted at 23-02-2026 08:26:00 AM is: The news reports that data from the Generator Statistical Digest 2025 confirms that all four units at Yallourn Power Station have ceased offering any of the ten FCAS markets since late 2024, a pattern that continued through 2025. This observation highlights a significant change in the power station's market participation, potentially impacting grid frequency control services in the National Electricity Market. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, from late 2024 through 2025 and beyond. Its root_cause is: Unknown (not specified in article; possibly related to operational or economic decisions). Its accident scale is: No accident reported. Its dates is: Starting late 2024, continuing through 2025 (as observed in GSD2025). Its affected region is: Yallourn Power Station, Victoria, Australia (within the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), electricity market participants, and grid operators relying on FCAS. Its cause type is: Operational change. Its causes is: Cessation of FCAS bidding by all four Yallourn units (YWPS1, YWPS2, YWPS3, YWPS4).

title: Belatedly noting return to service of YWPS2 (after rebuild/replacement of the LP turbine)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-08-26 YWPS2 unplanned outage due to 'LP turbine issues'
classified_content: The summary of the news "Belatedly noting return to service of YWPS2 (after rebuild/replacement of the LP turbine)" posted at 22-02-2026 04:04:00 PM is: Yallourn Power Station Unit 2 (YWPS2) returned to service on 16 February 2026 after a major unplanned outage beginning 26 August 2025, caused by low-pressure turbine blades breaking off and requiring full replacement of the LP turbine. Since returning, the unit has been running continuously and bidding at –$1,000/MWh, with no official communications from operator EnergyAustralia yet. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 26 August 2025 (outage start) to 16 February 2026 (return to service). Its root_cause is: LP turbine blade fracture causing extensive damage. Its accident scale is: Major outage requiring replacement of the LP turbine; no injuries reported. Its dates are: Outage start 26-08-2025, return to service 16-02-2026, news date 22-02-2026. Its affected region is: Yallourn, Victoria, Australia (part of the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely wholesale electricity market participants). Its cause type is: Mechanical failure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: LP turbine blades breaking off the shaft during operation.

title: Belatedly noting return to service of VP5 and VP6 after the double-unit trip
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Belatedly noting return to service of VP5 and VP6 after the double-unit trip" posted at 22-02-2026 03:30:00 PM is: This article belatedly reports the return to service of Vales Point units 5 and 6 (VP5 and VP6) following a double-unit trip that occurred around 2 February 2026. The simultaneous outages contributed to significant spot price volatility on 5–7 February 2026, alongside other factors like the Avon to Marulan transmission line constraint. VP6 returned on 6 February, while VP5 had a wobble—returning on 8 February, tripping again on 10 February, and finally returning on 14 February. As of 22 February, both units are back online. The article highlights AEMO communications and the impact of the outages on market prices.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Early February to mid-February 2026 (approximately 2 weeks). Its root_cause is: Unknown (the specific cause of the simultaneous trips is not identified). Its accident scale is: Two generating units (VP5 and VP6) tripped offline. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Main trip date: 02-02-2026; VP6 return: 06-02-2026; VP5 first return: 08-02-2026; VP5 second trip: 10-02-2026; VP5 final return: 14-02-2026. Its affected region is: Vales Point power station, New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) and electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Forced outage (trip). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Almost simultaneous trips of VP5 and VP6; specific root causes not disclosed.

title: An update on my home energy trading adventures in 10 charts
author: Andrew Wilson
topic: Personal experiences with Distributed Energy Resources
classified_content: The summary of the news "An update on my home energy trading adventures in 10 charts" posted at 20-02-2026 12:15:00 PM is: This article provides a personal, data-rich account of the author’s six-and-a-half-year experience with a home solar and battery system, focusing on 2025. After joining a wholesale price energy plan (Amber Electric) in mid-2023, the strategy shifted from self-sufficiency to optimizing cost vs. revenue by importing cheap grid power during low-price periods, using solar generation, and exporting battery surplus during evening peak prices. The author details energy flows, tariff impacts, EV charging costs averaging 2.6c/kWh after a network tariff update, and a net monthly bill of about $20. Total savings since installation are nearly $12,000 vs. a business-as-usual scenario, with around $250 per month savings recently. The piece also highlights the dominance of fixed charges and the volatility of earnings from price spike days. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: residential satisfaction. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Not applicable (personal experience, no market-wide event). Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 20-02-2026 (same as main date). Its affected region is: Australia (specifically Energex network area). Its affected key users is: The author’s household. Its cause type is: Not applicable. Its causes is: Not applicable.

title: Who killed FCAS?
author: Alex Leemon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Who killed FCAS?" posted at 19-02-2026 04:45:00 PM is: A detailed analysis of the collapse of Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) prices across all NEM markets from late 2023 through 2025, using data from GSD2025. The decline—with monthly average prices falling below $2/MW/h in many markets—is attributed to a rapid increase in registered FCAS capacity from grid-scale batteries, demand response, and virtual power plants, leading to oversupply in inherently shallow markets. Bidding trends show battery storage and DR/VPPs offering large volumes at the floor, while coal units also shifted to floor bids to stay enabled; hydro and gas largely did not. AEMO's previous procurement increases (e.g., in 2019) temporarily offset the rising capacity, but the later surge in registered units (several GW from batteries) overwhelmed the market. The article concludes that the key question is why FCAS prices were ever high, rather than who killed them. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since late 2023, persisting through 2025. Its root_cause is: Oversupply of FCAS capacity due to massive entry of new units (batteries, demand response, VPPs) coupled with structural bidding changes and shallow market depth. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident, only market price collapse). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Period from late 2023 to end of 2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), with New South Wales used as reference; all regions affected. Its affected key users is: FCAS market participants: battery operators, demand response providers, coal/hydro/gas generators, virtual power plants, and AEMO. Its cause type is: Market structural change (increase in registered capacity and bidding behavior). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Rapid deployment of utility-scale batteries from 2018 onward; (2) Entry of aggregated demand response and virtual power plants; (3) Increased bidding at floor price by batteries and coal units; (4) AEMO’s procurement volume increases in 2019 temporarily masking oversupply; (5) Removal of the multiplier effect in registration rules (October 2023) reducing registered volumes temporarily, but overall capacity still grew; (6) Retirement of some coal plants (e.g., Hazelwood) earlier reduced supply, but new entrants overwhelmed.

title: On 19th February 2026, AEMO declared Scheduling Error #74 (with respect to 16th October 2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "On 19th February 2026, AEMO declared Scheduling Error #74 (with respect to 16th October 2025)" posted at 19-02-2026 04:00:00 PM is: On 19 February 2026, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared a scheduling error (Incident #74) retroactive to 16 October 2025, when its Variable Renewable Energy Forecasting System (VREFS) incorrectly produced zero-megawatt forecasts for eight wind farms. Three of these wind farms used self-forecasts and were unaffected, but the remaining five wind farms (BANGOWF1, BANGOWF2, BODWF1, CROOKWF2, CRURWF1) were dispatched at zero megawatts for intervals between 12:30 and 15:10, with full rectification by 16:25 the same day. The incident was categorized as a SCADA/IT error and was permanently fixed by AEMO. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 16 October 2025 12:30 to 16:25 (3.92 hours). Its root_cause is: VREFS software error producing zero forecasts for eight wind farms. Its accident scale is: 5 wind farms dispatched at zero MW. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: Event date 16-10-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) – specific region not named. Its affected key users is: BANGOWF1, BANGOWF2, BODWF1, CROOKWF2, CRURWF1 wind farms. Its cause type is: SCADA/IT error. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: VREFS incorrectly generated 0 MW forecasts for 8 wind farms; 3 with self-forecasts not impacted; remaining 5 dispatched at zero.

title: A quick look at Pelican Point CCGT, with its recent upgrade
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "A quick look at Pelican Point CCGT, with its recent upgrade" posted at 11-02-2026 04:39:00 PM is: This article highlights the completion of a flexibility upgrade at the Pelican Point CCGT power station in South Australia, carried out between June and November 2025, which installed a mechanized diverter damper bypass stack to allow switching between open cycle (fast starts) and combined cycle (efficiency), alongside the adjacent 200 MW/400 MWh Pelican Point BESS. The upgrade aims to improve grid stability and responsiveness to changing demand. The article also references a statistical digest showing lower availability during 2025 but increased green energy bidding, and notes five periods of extreme price volatility between late December 2025 and early February 2026, as well as a ramp in availability starting January 22, 2026.

Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The upgrade's impact on market flexibility began after its completion in November 2025, with observable effects on availability from late January 2026 onward; extreme volatility events occurred from December 2025 through February 2026.  
Its root_cause is: The need for greater operational flexibility to support grid stability amid extreme weather and changing demand patterns in South Australia.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported).  
Its dates (if different) are: The main article date is 11-02-2026; the upgrade period was June to November 2025; the volatility events are noted on 28-12-2025, 06-01-2026, 18-01-2026, 26-01-2026, and 07-02-2026.  
Its affected region is: South Australia, Australia.  
Its affected key users is: Grid operators, electricity consumers, and market participants in South Australia.  
Its cause type is: Planned infrastructure upgrade.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Installation of a mechanized Diverter Damper Bypass Stack; conversion of one unit to open cycle for faster starts; construction of the adjacent Pelican Point BESS to complement gas flexibility; completion of upgrade works between June and November 2025.

title: I’m speaking next week at the Australian Power Symposium 2026 … ‘insights into the Australian spot electricity market’

title: Early afternoon price spikes in NSW on Thursday 5th February 2026
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Early afternoon price spikes in NSW on Thursday 5th February 2026" posted at 05-02-2026 03:15:00 PM is: On 5 February 2026, the New South Wales electricity market experienced unusual daylight price volatility, with spot prices spiking to the market price cap of $20,300/MWh at the 14:30 dispatch interval, followed by further elevated prices such as $7,380.81/MWh and $9,199.30/MWh. Sydney’s temperatures were in the low-to-mid 30s, consistent with a warm summer afternoon, but widespread cloud coverage across NSW was observed. Congestion maps showed concentrated negative local price adjustments and multiple binding mis-pricing constraints, indicating active constraints and material locational price separation. Forecast convergence for the evening showed shifting price forecasts, reflecting uncertainty in how conditions would evolve. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Immediate (afternoon of 5 February 2026). root_cause: Multiple binding constraints and congestion due to cloud coverage and locational price separation. accident scale: Unknown. dates: 05-02-2026. affected region: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. affected key users: Unknown. cause type: Operational constraints and congestion. causes: Widespread cloud coverage reducing solar generation, leading to network congestion and binding mis-pricing constraints.

title: NSW coal spot profits set to fall 30% to 40% thanks to batteries
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW coal spot profits set to fall 30% to 40% thanks to batteries" posted at 05-02-2026 01:20:00 PM is: The article analyzes how the impending influx of large-scale batteries in New South Wales (NSW) is expected to significantly reduce coal generation spot profits, with estimates of a 30% to 40% decline. It explains that while average spot prices may only drop slightly ($5–7/MWh), the primary impact comes from volume loss, as batteries cap evening peak prices at around $225/MWh and capture a portion of coal’s market share. Coal generators face the brunt of this displacement, but gas and hydro will also lose some volumes. The analysis concludes that even with futures prices near $100/MWh, the combination of lower volumes and high depreciation near end-of-life will materially erode accounting profits for coal plants. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: medium-term, ongoing as batteries are installed and begin operations. Its root_cause is: introduction of large-scale battery storage displacing coal generation during peak demand periods, reducing both peak prices and coal dispatch volumes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: same as main date (05-02-2026). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: coal-fired generators, gas-fired generators, and indirectly the wider market participants (though coal is the primary focus). Its cause type is: market competition from technology (battery storage). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Approximately 3.5 GW of new large-scale batteries coming online in NSW. 2. Batteries are expected to cap evening peak spot prices at no higher than $225/MWh. 3. Resulting reduction in coal generation volumes and revenues, leading to a 30–40% drop in notional spot profits.

title: The NEM’s first solar-battery hybrid, Quorn Park, enters testing
author: Adam Kent
classified_content: The summary of the news "The NEM’s first solar-battery hybrid, Quorn Park, enters testing" posted at 05-02-2026 12:26:00 PM is: The Quorn Park Solar Hybrid, a 98 MW solar farm paired with a 20 MW battery, has begun testing as the first true hybrid generator to connect to the NEM. Registered under AEMO’s 2024 Aggregated Dispatch rules as a mixed aggregate system (solar as semi-scheduled, battery as scheduled), it uses the BESS to firm intermittent solar output or charge from curtailed generation. During initial testing, a non-conformance notice (MN 133250) was issued on 3 February 2026 for a 10 MW deviation over 20 minutes in NSW1, likely due to hold point testing. This milestone marks the start of many such hybrids expected online. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (20-minute non-conformance on 3 February 2026). Its root_cause is: Hold point testing during commissioning of the new hybrid generator. Its accident scale is: 10 MW non-conformance for 20 minutes. Its dates is: 03-02-2026. Its affected region is: NSW1 (New South Wales). Its affected key users is: Quorn Park Solar Hybrid (QPSFB1 solar unit, QPSFB2 BESS unit) and AEMO. Its cause type is: Operational/commissioning testing. Its causes is: Hold point testing of aggregate dispatch group leading to a non-conformance with dispatch target or constraint.

title: Keeping up with the curtailment 2025: Beneath the headline numbers
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Keeping up with the curtailment 2025: Beneath the headline numbers" posted at 04-02-2026 07:00:00 AM is: This article analyzes curtailment patterns for solar and wind generators in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) during 2025, using data from the Generator Statistical Digest. It distinguishes between network curtailment (due to transmission constraints) and economic curtailment (due to negative prices), noting that both forms rose sharply year-on-year—total curtailment reached 7.2 TWh in 2025, up from 4.3 TWh in 2024. Regional divergences are highlighted: Queensland and South Australia/Victoria see high economic curtailment in spring/summer, while New South Wales has lower economic curtailment but higher network curtailment, especially due to bottlenecks like the 94T constraint. The article also points to growing impacts of transmission outages (e.g., Crowlands Wind Farm) and the structural increase in zero/negative price intervals, making asset performance increasingly challenging beyond headline averages.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing (2025 data) with continued impact into future. Its root_cause is: High VRE penetration combined with network constraints, low demand during shoulder seasons, and increased frequency of zero/negative prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions of Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia. Its affected key users is: Solar and wind farm developers, operators, and investors; also system operators and market participants. Its cause type is: Network constraints and market pricing effects. Its causes is: Rising zero/negative price intervals, binding transmission line limits (e.g., Molong-Orange 132 kV, Wagga-Walla Walla 330 kV outage), seasonal demand troughs, and high distributed PV penetration.

title: Coal is adjusting but the market isn’t waiting
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "Coal is adjusting but the market isn’t waiting" posted at 03-02-2026 01:34:00 PM is: This article examines the changing role of coal generation in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting how aging coal plants like Mt Piper and Bayswater have adapted to increased flexibility demands but are now experiencing frequent outages during critical peak periods (e.g., extended outages in April‑May 2025). The closure of Liddell in 2023, the narrowing revenue window (with 10% of revenue now earned in the 7‑8 PM slot), and rising competition from batteries (capacity set to triple) and renewables are eroding coal profitability. The market is responding with price signals that incentivize new battery investment, while coal units struggle with operational risks and deteriorating economics, leading to an inevitable but bumpy transition. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: ongoing (2025‑2026 and the next couple of years). Its root_cause is: aging coal plants forced into flexible operation, combined with increased competition from batteries and renewables, resulting in outages and reduced profitability. Its accident scale is: multiple unit outages (Mt Piper Unit 1, Bayswater Unit 3) with extended durations (weeks to months). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 2025 (outages in April‑May 2025; Liddell closure in 2023). Its affected region is: Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly New South Wales (Mt Piper, Bayswater, Liddell), Victoria, and Queensland. Its affected key users is: electricity consumers in the NEM, coal generator operators (e.g., AGL, Origin), and battery developers. Its cause type is: technical (aging infrastructure) and market/economic (changing dispatch signals, revenue compression, competition). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Ageing coal plants designed for baseload now required to cycle flexibly; 2. Outages at Mt Piper (Unit 1, Apr‑May 2025) and Bayswater (Unit 3, May 2025) during peak demand; 3. Closure of Liddell in 2023; 4. Narrowing of profitable price windows (evening peak); 5. Rapid growth of battery storage (GW tripling, GWh quadrupling); 6. Increasing price‑insensitive wind and solar output; 7. Economics showing coal costs of $60‑70/MWh under threat from lower‑cost competition.

title: Cutting through the noise
author: Oliver Nunn
classified_content: The summary of the news "Cutting through the noise" posted at 27-01-2026 01:55:00 PM is: This article analyzes the increasing "noise" (transient oscillations) in NEM spot prices, measured as average absolute variation between consecutive 5-minute dispatch intervals. Variation has risen from ~$1/MWh in 2010 to >$10/MWh in 2025, with a clear intraday structure: positive spikes occur in the last 5 minutes of each half-hour, and negative spikes in the first 5 minutes. The root cause is identified as a structural mismatch – most thermal generators (e.g., black coal) bid on a half-hourly basis, while batteries and some hydro bid on 5-minute intervals, combined with increased variability in demand and VRE. This noise enhances arbitrage opportunities for short-duration batteries but also makes perfect trading harder. The article stresses that investors must model noise to assess project viability and that the phenomenon may be either a sophisticated market signal or a pathological bidding outcome. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with historical data from 2010 to 2025 and future implications for market behavior and investment. Its root_cause is: Structural mismatch between half-hourly thermal generator bids and 5-minute settlement intervals, combined with increased variability in demand and variable renewable energy (VRE) supply. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 27-01-2026 01:55:00 PM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, with particular focus on NSW as a case study. Its affected key users is: Market participants, investors, battery operators, generators, and regulatory bodies. Its cause type is: Market design and bidding behavior / structural market issue. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Thermal generators (black coal) continue to submit bids on a half-hourly basis. 2. Batteries and some hydro generators provide bids on 5-minute intervals. 3. Increased variability in demand and VRE (solar, wind) creates more frequent supply-demand imbalance. 4. Bidding structures have not fully adapted to the 5-minute settlement framework, leading to predictable intra-half-hour price oscillations.

title: How close to empty did South Australia’s utility-scale battery fleet run last night?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "How close to empty did South Australia’s utility-scale battery fleet run last night?" posted at 27-01-2026 11:17:00 AM is: South Australia's utility-scale battery fleet nearly depleted its stored energy during the evening of 26 January 2026, as extreme heat pushed demand within a few hundred megawatts of the region's record. Aggregate stored energy fell from a peak of 1,325 MWh to just 66 MWh, with major batteries like Torrens Island (250 MW) and Blyth (200 MW) becoming unavailable while prices were at the Market Price Cap of $20,300/MWh, leaving minimal discharge capability for the remainder of the high-price window. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Evening of 26 January 2026 through early morning of 27 January 2026. Its root_cause is: Historically high temperatures driving elevated market demand to near-record levels, sustained for over three hours. Its accident scale is: Battery fleet stored energy dropped from 1,325 MWh to 66 MWh, effectively reducing discharge capability to near zero. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-01-2026. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather (extreme heat). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: High temperatures; near-record demand; sustained high electricity prices for over three hours; batteries discharging to minimum state-of-charge; Torrens Island and Blyth BESS becoming unavailable from around 20:40-20:45 NEM time.

title: SA sees a long period of sustained high prices near the MPC amid 40°C heat on the evening of Australia Day 2026
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "SA sees a long period of sustained high prices near the MPC amid 40°C heat on the evening of Australia Day 2026" posted at 26-01-2026 08:54:00 PM is: On the evening of Australia Day 2026, South Australia experienced a sustained period of very high spot prices near the Market Price Cap (MPC) due to extreme 40°C heat driving elevated air-conditioning demand through the evening peak. Regional demand reached 3,140 MW, tracking above earlier forecasts, and AEMO issued an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) condition from 20:30 to 22:00 hrs, indicating tight supply reserves. The heat persisted longer than anticipated, pushing prices to MPC territory while available generation remained flat. This combination of extreme weather and high demand led to significant market price spikes and a system reliability alert. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (evening of 26 January 2026, lasting several hours). Its root_cause is: Extreme heat (40°C) causing high air-conditioning load that pushed demand above forecasts and strained generation reserves. Its accident scale is: Minor (LOR1 condition, no physical failure or customer disconnection reported). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 26-01-2026. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA) region. Its affected key users is: Residential and commercial electricity consumers in SA (facing high spot prices and potential load-shedding risk). Its cause type is: Weather-driven demand surge. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Extreme 40°C heat persisting into the evening; 2) Strong air-conditioning load despite public holiday; 3) Demand exceeding P30 forecasts; 4) Available generation remaining relatively flat; 5) Insufficient capacity reserve leading to LOR1 declaration.

title: Exploring the potential impacts of HumeLink and Snowy 2.0 on the Southern NSW electricity network
author: Ryan Powell
classified_content: The summary of the news "Exploring the potential impacts of HumeLink and Snowy 2.0 on the Southern NSW electricity network" posted at 22-01-2026 03:41:00 PM is: A modelling analysis using EPEC’s integrated PLEXOS & PSSE model investigates how the HumeLink transmission project and Snowy 2.0 pumped hydro scheme will affect the southern NSW network. HumeLink (expected July 2029) is forecast to alleviate existing congestion on the 132kV lines between Wagga and Yass and slightly ease central NSW constraints. However, after Snowy 2.0 becomes fully operational in July 2030, new congestion is predicted on the Cowra–Bango 132kV line and the Wagga–Lower Tumut 330kV line due to Snowy 2.0’s pumping load. Additionally, coal retirements are expected to increase congestion on flow paths into Sydney. The study identifies key network considerations requiring further exploration. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: July 2029 (HumeLink commissioning) to beyond 2032 (post-Snowy 2.0). Its root_cause is: The need to integrate Snowy 2.0’s pumping load and manage existing transmission congestion, leading to new constraint patterns. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 22-01-2026 (news date); event dates: July 2029 (HumeLink), July 2030 (Snowy 2.0). Its affected region is: Southern NSW electricity network (including areas around Wagga, Yass, Cowra, Bango, and Sydney inflow paths). Its affected key users are: Transmission system operators, generators (including Snowy 2.0 and solar farms), and consumers in southern NSW. Its cause type is: Transmission constraints and generation integration. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Existing 132kV congestion between Wagga and Yass from high solar generation and imports; Snowy 2.0 pumping load increasing flows on Cowra–Bango and Wagga–Lower Tumut lines; NSW coal retirements adding pressure on Sydney inflow paths; network reconfiguration with HumeLink.

title: Unplanned outage on the Balranald- Darlington point X5 220 kV line, and Balranald to Buronga (X3) 220kV line in southern NSW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unplanned outage on the Balranald- Darlington point X5 220 kV line, and Balranald to Buronga (X3) 220kV line in southern NSW" posted at 21-01-2026 10:16:00 AM is: On 19 January 2026, an unplanned outage of two 220 kV transmission lines (Balranald-Darlington Point X5 and Balranald-Buronga X3) in southern NSW triggered a non-credible contingency event, disconnecting approximately 3 MW of bulk electrical load. The outage is expected to last until at least 4 February 2026, potentially affecting variable renewable energy curtailment and inter-regional economic islanding. The exact cause remains unknown. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 19 January 2026 to 4 February 2026 (approximately 16 days). Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: 3 MW of bulk electrical load disconnected; two 220 kV lines outaged. Its dates is: 19-01-2026. Its affected region is: Southern NSW, Australia (Balranald, Darlington Point, Buronga). Its affected key users is: Unknown (includes bulk electrical load and interconnector users). Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: Trip of Balranald-Darlington Point X5 and Balranald-Buronga X3 220 kV lines at 21:14 on 19 January 2026.

title: Semi-dispatch caps are loading the deviation dice
author: Allan O'Neil
topic: Is the Semi-Scheduled Category Sustainable or Scalable?
classified_content: The summary of the news "Semi-dispatch caps are loading the deviation dice" posted at 21-01-2026 08:39:00 AM is: The article explains how semi-dispatch caps (SDC) on semi-scheduled VRE generators in Australia’s NEM create a systematic asymmetry, forcing capped units to only deviate downward from dispatch targets, leading to persistent aggregate under-performance and frequency drift issues. It illustrates with data from 16 January 2026 and long-term averages, highlighting implications for frequency control and primary frequency response (PFR). Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Grid frequency issues). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with specific example on 16 Jan 2026. Its root_cause is: Semi-dispatch cap rules that prohibit upward deviations when SDC is on, combined with forecast variability in VRE availability. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (no accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 21-01-2026 (article date), with example data from 16 Jan 2026. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled VRE generators (wind, solar), AEMO (grid operator), frequency control markets. Its cause type is: Regulatory/design flaw. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Semi-dispatch caps prevent upward deviations for capped units, only allowing downward deviations. 2. Dispatch availability forecasts are 5-minute ahead and inherently variable. 3. Asymmetry leads to aggregate under-delivery when a significant share of VRE is capped, as upward deviations from uncapped units are insufficient to offset.

title: Under-performance of Wind Farms increased during the recent heatwave
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news “Under-performance of Wind Farms increased during the recent heatwave” posted at 20-01-2026 02:29:00 PM is: During a January 2026 heatwave, the aggregate dispatch error (target minus actual output) for semi-scheduled wind farms across the National Electricity Market (NEM) increased significantly on hot days from 7 to 10 January, with under-performance notably higher in South Australia, Victoria, and New South Wales due to high-temperature effects on turbine output and over-optimistic forecasts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 07-01-2026 to 10-01-2026 (the heatwave period). Its root_cause is: High temperatures during the heatwave caused reduced wind farm output (under-performance relative to dispatch targets). Its accident scale is: Unknown (the event is a systemic under-performance across the entire semi-scheduled wind farm fleet, not a singular accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-01-2026 to 10-01-2026. Its affected region is: South Australia, Victoria, New South Wales (as explicitly mentioned for each hot day). Its affected key users is: Electricity market operators, wind farm operators, and grid reliability managers. Its cause type is: Weather-related (heatwave). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: High ambient temperatures reducing wind turbine efficiency and output; over-optimistic forecasting leading to positive dispatch error; semi-dispatch cap arrangements exacerbating under-performance.

title: The long goodbye continues as Eraring’s closure officially extended until 2029
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "The long goodbye continues as Eraring’s closure officially extended until 2029" posted at 20-01-2026 11:30:00 AM is: Origin Energy has formally extended the operating life of the Eraring coal-fired power station in New South Wales from August 2027 to April 2029, citing the need to ensure grid stability and reliable supply amid delays in renewable energy, storage, and transmission projects. The announcement follows earlier warnings from the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) about potential reliability shortfalls in 2027-2028, which the extension is expected to resolve, as confirmed by subsequent AEMO market notices that no longer identify low reserve conditions for that period.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: Grid/system instability.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From August 2027 to April 2029 (the extended operational period), with immediate market impacts on reliability forecasts published on 20-21 January 2026.; Its root_cause is: Insufficient progress in delivering new renewable energy, storage, and transmission infrastructure, combined with uncertainty about the reliability of Australia’s aging coal and gas fleet, leading to a need for continued coal-fired generation to maintain system security.; Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident occurred; the event is a planned operational extension).; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 20-01-2026 (date of announcement); extension effective from 19 August 2027 to 30 April 2029.; Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia.; Its affected key users are: Households and businesses in NSW, as well as the broader National Electricity Market (NEM).; Its cause type is: Planned operational extension of a coal-fired power station.; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Delays in constructing new renewable energy projects, large-scale batteries, and transmission lines; (2) AEMO’s identification of potential unserved energy exceeding the reliability standard in NSW for 2027-2028; (3) Origin’s assessment of market conditions and customer needs; (4) Uncertainty regarding the reliability of other aging coal and gas units in Australia.

title: Callide C4 begins its return to service journey
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2026-01-15 double unit trip at Callide C
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C4 begins its return to service journey" posted at 20-01-2026 10:53:00 AM is: On 20 January 2026, Callide C4 unit returned to service following a double unit trip at Callide C power station on 15 January 2026, which was classified as a non-credible contingency event in the Queensland region. CS Energy attributed the trip to human error and implemented preventive measures; AEMO confirmed the cause was identified and another occurrence is unlikely. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 15 January 2026 to 20 January 2026 (impact from trip until return to service). Its root_cause is: human error. Its accident scale is: Double unit trip (two units at Callide C). Its dates (if different from the main date) are: 15-01-2026 (event date) and 20-01-2026 (update date). Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO, CS Energy, electricity consumers in QLD. Its cause type is: human error. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Human error during operations leading to coincident trip of two generating units.

title: Callide C3 starts RTS on Monday afternoon 19th January 2026
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C3 starts RTS on Monday afternoon 19th January 2026" posted at 19-01-2026 01:55:00 PM is: After a control system fault caused both Callide C3 and C4 units to trip from around 800 MW to zero on 15 January 2026, Unit C3 began its return to service on the afternoon of 19 January, reaching near full capacity by 17:30 NEM time, while Unit C4 remains offline with expected restoration the following morning. Its **relevance level** is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its **category** is: turbine/generator failures. Its **Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market** is: Short-term (days), with impact from the trip on 15 Jan to the staggered return of units on 19-20 Jan. Its **root_cause** is: Control system fault. Its **accident scale** is: Two generating units (C3 and C4) tripped simultaneously from 800 MW to zero, resulting in a precautionary evacuation; C3 restored to 424 MW of 466 MW capacity, C4 still offline. Its **dates** is: 15 January 2026 (trip event), 19 January 2026 (C3 return), 20 January 2026 (C4 expected return). Its **affected region** is: Queensland, Australia (Callide Power Station region). Its **affected key users** is: National Electricity Market (NEM) participants, grid operators, and downstream consumers dependent on supply. Its **cause type** is: Technical/control system fault. Its **causes** is: Control system fault leading to the coincident trip of both Callide C3 and C4 units.

title: Stress-testing the NEM
author: Oliver Nunn
classified_content: The summary of the news "Stress-testing the NEM" posted at 19-01-2026 12:30:00 PM is: This article argues that current deterministic modeling based on median weather and demand assumptions is inadequate for understanding the variability, uncertainty, and reliability risks in the National Electricity Market (NEM) as renewable penetration increases. By stress-testing the system across 13 weather reference years and three demand-supply sensitivities, the analysis reveals wide distributions of revenue per MW for assets (e.g., gas generation can earn super-normal profits in some years and nothing in others), large swings in gas consumption (up to 120 PJ across southern states), and extreme unserved energy events (e.g., 50–100 times the reliability standard in NSW by the 2040s) under plausible conditions. The authors recommend adopting distribution-based modeling, stress testing the entire energy supply chain, and developing market mechanisms to reward insurance-style assets that provide resilience during outlier events. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (ongoing, with projections to the 2040s). Its root_cause is: The inherent stochastic variability of weather and demand-supply conditions coupled with high renewable penetration, which deterministic models fail to capture. Its accident scale is: Unknown (the analysis describes potential outcomes, not a specific accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 19-01-2026. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, with specific examples for NSW. Its affected key users is: Generators (wind, solar, gas, batteries), gas supply systems, consumers (via reliability and unserved energy), market operators, and planners. Its cause type is: Stochastic variability (weather, demand, supply). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Weather variability across 13 reference years, (2) Demand-supply sensitivities (N-1, N+1 relative to forecast), (3) High penetration of variable renewable energy (VRE) leading to long-tailed distributions of outcomes.

title: One hour of NEM mainland frequency performance, on Friday 16th January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "One hour of NEM mainland frequency performance, on Friday 16th January 2026" posted at 16-01-2026 03:06:00 PM is: A one-hour snapshot of frequency performance in the National Electricity Market (NEM) mainland on Friday 16 January 2026 at 15:00 NEM time highlights ongoing frequency control issues, following the previous day's simultaneous trip of Callide C3 and C4 units. The article suggests that variable renewable energy (VRE) under-performance may be dragging the frequency down, indicating persistent grid instability concerns. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 1-hour period on 16 January 2026 at 15:00 NEM time. Its root_cause is: Under-performance of variable renewable energy (VRE) causing frequency deviations, compounded by insufficient frequency control. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 16-01-2026 (same as main date). Its affected region is: NEM mainland (mainland states of the Australian National Electricity Market, excluding Tasmania). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational/Technical – frequency control issue. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: VRE under-performance; insufficient frequency control response; prior day's Callide C3 and C4 trip as a contextual reminder of system vulnerability.

classified_content: The summary of the news "CS Energy issues ‘Statement of Outage’ that points to ‘control system fault’ (and mentions precautionary evacuation)" posted at 16-01-2026 02:56:00 PM is: CS Energy reported a control system fault causing coincident trip of both Callide C units, leading to a precautionary evacuation of personnel coordinated with Powerlink Queensland, with the return-to-service timeline uncertain. Its affected key users is: Electricity generators, Powerlink Queensland (transmission operator), and NEM market participants. Its cause type is: Technical/control system fault. title: Helpful and unhelpful MW deviations on 15th January 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Helpful and unhelpful MW deviations on 15th January 2025" posted at 16-01-2026 02:47:00 PM is: On 15th January 2025, the simultaneous trip of Callide C3 and C4 units at 16:55 caused a frequency decline in the NEM. Analysis of unit deviations from target trajectories shows the tripped units were the largest unhelpful contributors, along with several underperforming solar farms. Helpful deviations came from the Western Downs BESS (over 150 MW), Waratah BESS, Vic Big Battery, and coal units like Bayswater, while the Bouldercombe battery was technically limited. The frequency drop lasted into the 17:05 interval. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures and grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact from 16:55 to 17:05 on 15th January 2025. Its root_cause is: Simultaneous trip of Callide C3 and C4 generating units. Its accident scale is: Two units tripped, causing a system frequency drop and aggregate negative deviation of 1,500 MW. Its dates are: 15-01-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (all NEM participants). Its cause type is: Generator failure (unit trip). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Trip of Callide C3; Trip of Callide C4.

title: Was it the simultaneous trip of Callide C (or was it something else!) that put the most pressure on System Frequency on Thursday afternoon 15th January 2025?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Was it the simultaneous trip of Callide C (or was it something else!) that put the most pressure on System Frequency on Thursday afternoon 15th January 2025?" posted at 16-01-2026 12:59:00 PM is: On Thursday 15 January 2026, the simultaneous trip of Callide C3 and C4 coal units caused a sharp frequency drop that initially stayed within the normal operating frequency band but later fell outside. The aggregate underperformance from these units was 802 MW, partially offset by other coal units (427 MW over-performance), resulting in a net coal underperformance of 375 MW. Alarmingly, large solar units also underperformed by 859 MW, contributing to frequency weakness. Batteries, hydro, and gas provided helpful responses. The article highlights concerns about the lack of corrective action regarding variable renewable energy underperformance and regulation FCAS.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (day of event). Its root_cause is: Simultaneous trip of Callide C3 and C4 units. Its accident scale is: Two units tripped, 802 MW underperformance. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 15-01-2026. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO, grid operators. Its cause type is: technical failure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Simultaneous trip of Callide C3 and C4.

title: Charting the 4-second data following trip of Callide C3 and C4
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Charting the 4-second data following trip of Callide C3 and C4" posted at 16-01-2026 10:41:00 AM is: This article analyses the simultaneous trip of the Callide C3 and C4 units, which caused an immediate frequency drop from 400 MW to 0 MW, reaching 49.866 Hz initially then further declining to 49.831 Hz within five minutes. The event occurred just after 16:55, aligning with the start of a dispatch interval, and required FCAS and primary frequency response to arrest the decline until new unit targets were set at 17:00, with frequency correction beginning around 24 seconds into the interval due to unit ramping. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate frequency disturbance lasting several minutes until re-dispatch at the next interval. Its root_cause is: Simultaneous trip of Callide C3 and C4 generating units. Its accident scale is: Two units tripped, each from 400 MW to 0 MW, total 800 MW loss. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Event date 15-01-2026 (per topic), post date 16-01-2026. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (Callide Power Station region). Its affected key users is: Grid operators, frequency control providers, and end consumers. Its cause type is: Unplanned generator trip. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Simultaneous trip of Callide C3 and C4; specific underlying reason not stated in article.

title: Quickly following the trip of Callide C3 and C4 (a first look at rebidding onsite)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Quickly following the trip of Callide C3 and C4 (a first look at rebidding onsite)" posted at 16-01-2026 09:05:00 AM is: The article examines rebid data for Callide C3 and C4 after their coincident trips on 15 January 2026 but finds no cause revealed in the bids, noting previous major incidents (C4 turbo-alternator explosion, C3 cooling tower failure) and reduced availability on B1 and B2 due to mill limits that may have contributed to price spikes; the root cause remains unknown, whether from onsite, network, or other factors. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact from 15 January 2026, with outages expected until at least 17 January 2026 (Saturday evening) per return-to-service expectations. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Two generating units (Callide C3 and C4) tripped, and two others (B1 and B2) had reduced availability. Its dates is: 15-16 January 2026 (event on 15th, article on 16th). Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants and consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Unknown (technical failure suspected but not determined). Its causes is: Coincident trips of Callide C3 and C4; reduced availability of Callide B1 and B2 due to mill limits.

title: *Speculating on* a possible trigger for the coincident trip of Callide C3 and C4 (related to the ’Q-TR_CLHA_-600’ constraint set?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Speculating on a possible trigger for the coincident trip of Callide C3 and C4 (related to the ’Q-TR_CLHA_-600’ constraint set?)" posted at 15-01-2026 10:36:00 PM is: A speculative analysis suggests that the coincident trip of Callide C3 and C4 units at 16:55 NEM time on 15 January 2026 may be linked to the earlier invocation of the 'Q-TR_CLHA_-600' constraint set (08:05-19:00), which relates to a voltage stability limit at the Calvale substation where Callide connects. The constraint had been limiting generation from Callide units. Short-term reductions in availability at Callide B1 and B2 were also noted. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Initial event at 16:55 on 15 Jan 2026; duration unknown. Its root_cause is: Speculated to be related to voltage stability constraint invocation, but unconfirmed. Its accident scale is: Coincident trip of two large units (Callide C3 and C4) and reduced output from two additional units (Callide B1 and B2). Its dates is: 15-01-2026. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (Callide Power Station and Calvale substation). Its affected key users is: NEM participants, Queensland consumers, AEMO. Its cause type is: Technical/Operational (speculative). Its causes is: Invocation of constraint set 'Q-TR_CLHA_-600' due to voltage stability limit at Calvale; possible common mode failure.

title: Coincident with the trip of Callide C3 and C4 (reduced availability for B1 and B2)

The event was observed via the Forecast Convergence widget in ez2view at 21:15 NEM time on 15 January.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: turbine/generator failures.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 15 January 2026 until at least 17 January 2026 (expected return of C3 and C4).; Its root_cause is: Unknown, but possibly related to a common cause affecting both C and B units.; Its accident scale is: Two generating units (Callide C3 and C4) tripped, and two additional units (Callide B1 and B2) experienced reduced availability.; Its dates is: Event date: 15-01-2026; expected return date: 17-01-2026.; Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (Callide power station).; Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, including the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) and NEM generators/retailers.; Its cause type is: Unplanned generator trip/failure.; Its causes is: Trip of Callide C3 and C4; reduced availability of Callide B1 and B2 (possibly secondary effect).

title: Quickly following the trip of Callide C3 and C4 (initial pricing impacts)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Quickly following the trip of Callide C3 and C4 (initial pricing impacts)" posted at 15-01-2026 10:10:00 PM is: The article details the unexpected simultaneous trip of Callide C3 and C4 coal units in Queensland on 15 January 2026 at approximately 16:55 NEM time, resulting in a loss of about 800 MW. A detailed analysis of dispatch intervals from 16:50 to 17:25 reveals that the trip did not cause immediate major price spikes; the first substantive effect was seen in the 17:05 dispatch interval, but actual prices were lower than forecasts. Significant price increases above $1,000/MWh occurred roughly two hours later, likely due to rebidding of the units' capacity. The immediate impact on dispatch intervals was limited and not directly reflected in price jumps.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to 2 hours after trip on 15-01-2026, with major price spikes emerging around 2 hours post-trip. Its root_cause is: Unexpected trip of Callide C3 and C4 coal-fired units. Its accident scale is: Two units tripped, losing approximately 800 MW. Its dates is: 15-01-2026. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unplanned outage / generator trip. Its causes is: Simultaneous trip of Callide C3 and C4 at 16:55:21.7 NEM time.

title: 4 hours after the trip of Callide C3 and C4 (return to service expectation pushed back to Saturday evening 17th January 2026)

title: Looking further into battery operations during NSW volatility on Saturday 10th January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Looking further into battery operations during NSW volatility on Saturday 10th January 2026" posted at 15-01-2026 08:20:00 AM is: The article analyzes battery operations in NSW on 10 January 2026 during a price volatility event, finding that many batteries did not reach peak output during the initial price spike above $10,000/MWh at the 18:35 dispatch interval, but instead six dispatch intervals later (19:05), primarily due to a binding constraint equation 'N>>16_8_39' caused by a long-running network outage on the Avon to Marulan 330kV line, which constrained off some batteries; additionally, rebidding behavior by operators like Eraring Battery contributed to the delayed ramp-up. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 10 January 2026, during the volatile period from approximately 18:35 to 19:05 NEM time. Its root_cause is: Long-running network outage on the Avon to Marulan 330kV line leading to constraint equation 'N>>16_8_39'. Its accident scale is: Network outage (non-accident, planned/unplanned outage). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 10-01-2026. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Battery operators, AEMO, electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Network constraint/Outage. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Long-running network outage on Avon to Marulan 330kV line; 2) Binding constraint equation 'N>>16_8_39' limiting battery output; 3) Rebidding behavior by some battery operators (e.g., Eraring Battery shifting volume to out-of-money bands).

title: Eternal sunshine: Recent and projected patterns in solar performance
author: Oliver Nunn
classified_content: The summary of the news "Eternal sunshine: Recent and projected patterns in solar performance" posted at 14-01-2026 11:45:00 AM is: The article analyzes solar performance in the National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting that average prices fell in 2025 compared to 2024 but rose from 2023 levels, with New South Wales averaging $103/MWh and Victoria $77.9/MWh. The deepening duck curve has caused solar-hour prices to collapse, often turning negative in recent quarters, while solar revenue is increasingly earned during winter shoulder periods when output is low. Projections to 2040 show solar earning virtually nothing from September to March, with revenue becoming highly seasonal and volatile, necessitating seasonal storage or gas for hedging. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing and long-term (historical from 2010 to 2025, projected to 2040). Its root_cause is: Increased penetration of solar (especially rooftop solar) leading to oversupply during sunny hours and a structural shift in price patterns. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 14-01-2026. Its affected region is: Mainland regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM), with detailed analysis on Victoria. Its affected key users is: Solar farm investors, financiers, grid operators, and energy policymakers. Its cause type is: Market dynamics (renewable penetration). Its causes is: Increased solar capacity, particularly rooftop solar; seasonal and diurnal demand patterns; lack of sufficient seasonal storage; resulting in collapse of solar-hour prices and revenue concentration in winter.
