title: A brief overview of the situation in NSW on Saturday 10th January 2026 (prior to further analysis)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A brief overview of the situation in NSW on Saturday 10th January 2026 (prior to further analysis)" posted at 13-01-2026 03:27:00 PM is: On 10 January 2026, NSW experienced high evening demand and extreme price volatility, with the spot price spiking to $11,923.60/MWh at 18:35, driven by a combination of factors including solar sunset, low wind output, constrained interconnectors unable to import more power, a ramp-up in coal/gas/hydro/battery generation, and a delayed battery response. The event led to a time-weighted average price near $500/MWh, causing financial pain for spot-exposed energy users and short wholesale participants. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 10 January 2026 (the day of the event), with the most volatile period from 18:00 to 20:00 NEM time. Its root_cause is: A combination of high underlying demand, the evening decline in solar generation, low wind output, interconnector flow constraints into NSW, and a slower-than-expected battery ramp-up, which together triggered a supply-demand imbalance and extreme price spikes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: Event date 10-01-2026; news posted 13-01-2026. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Spot-exposed energy users (who could not curtail) and wholesale participants short on generation. Its cause type is: Operational/system conditions. Its causes is: High market demand (peak 13,147 MW at 18:35); solar generation near zero at sunset; relatively low wind output; all three interconnectors constrained on flows into NSW; batteries ramping up with a delay relative to the price spike; reliance on coal, gas, and hydro ramp-up.

title: Data centres and the new energy reality
author: Mitch O'Neill
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Data centres and the new energy reality" posted at 13-01-2026 is: The article details the rapid growth in electricity demand from AI data centres in Australia, particularly NSW, driven by next-generation GPUs with rack power densities rising from 26 kW to as high as 600 kW. Transgrid’s 2025 planning report forecasts data centre load at 6,723 GWh by 2035, roughly 10% of total NSW demand, replacing earlier hydrogen projections. Changes in data centre design (liquid cooling, denser electrical infrastructure), location constraints in Sydney’s Eastern Creek area, and uncertainties around the AI bubble and project financing are discussed. This demand surge will significantly influence electricity market planning and prices.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2.  
Its category is: market reactions.  
Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (up to 2035).  
Its root_cause is: Increased AI data centre demand due to higher GPU power densities and networking requirements that pack more chips in close proximity.  
Its accident scale is: N/A.  
Its dates are: 13-01-2026.  
Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia, particularly the Eastern Creek area of Sydney.  
Its affected key users are: Data centre operators, electricity grid operators (Transgrid, AEMO), and energy consumers.  
Its cause type is: Demand growth (structural shift).  
Its causes are: Increased GPU power density across generations (e.g., from 26 kW in 2020 to 600 kW by 2027), networking advantages of copper interconnects requiring tight chip packing, rapid AI adoption, and short construction timelines forcing design changes.

title: TAS energy and FCAS prices spike on Monday afternoon, 12th January 2026
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "TAS energy and FCAS prices spike on Monday afternoon, 12th January 2026" posted at 12-01-2026 06:51:00 PM is: On the afternoon of 12 January 2026, the Tasmanian (TAS) region experienced a brief but intense price spike, with the spot price and four Raise FCAS commodities (6 Sec, 60 Sec, 5 Min, and Regulation) surging to or near the Market Price Cap (MPC) for the 16:55 and 17:00 NEM dispatch intervals. The spike coincided with the Basslink interconnector’s import and export limits dropping to 0 MW after flowing southward for the previous 32 hours, triggered by constraint equations T_BLINK_VT_BID (setting import limit to zero when the interconnector is bid zero) and T_BLINK_VT_NGZ (preventing sign changes in the interconnector target within a single dispatch interval).; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: Grid/system instability.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes to hours, limited to two dispatch intervals).; Its root_cause is: Basslink interconnector import and export limits suddenly set to 0 MW due to operational constraint equations.; Its accident scale is: Unknown (no physical accident reported; event was a price spike of short duration).; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 12-01-2026.; Its affected region is: Tasmania (TAS region of the Australian National Electricity Market).; Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely market participants and consumers in TAS, but not specified).; Its cause type is: Operational/constraint (interconnector bidding and limit calculation).; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Basslink interconnector flow dropped to 0 MW in both directions simultaneously. 2) Constraint equation T_BLINK_VT_BID limited import to 0 MW due to zero bidding. 3) Constraint equation T_BLINK_VT_NGZ limited export to 0 MW to avoid sign change of interconnector target.

title: Long-running network outage on Avon to Marulan 330kV line in summer 2025-26
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Long-running network outage on Avon to Marulan 330kV line in summer 2025-26" posted at 12-01-2026 08:10:00 AM is: This article describes a prolonged outage on the Avon to Marulan 330kV transmission line that triggers the 'N-AVMN_16' constraint set, containing three constraint equations. The outage is scheduled from at least 10 January 2026 through mid-March 2026, and was already affecting market outcomes on Saturday 10 January 2026. TransGrid's reasons for the outage are not detailed, but the constraint set remains invoked over this extended period.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 10 January 2026 to mid-March 2026; Its root_cause is: outage on the Avon to Marulan 330kV transmission line; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates (if different from the main date) are: 10 January 2026 (event start), 12 January 2026 (report date); Its affected region is: likely New South Wales, Australia; Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: network outage (transmission line); Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: outage of the Avon to Marulan 330kV line leading to invocation of constraint set N-AVMN_16.

title: The Victoria Big Battery was activated for SIPS on Friday afternoon 9th January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Victoria Big Battery was activated for SIPS on Friday afternoon 9th January 2026" posted at 10-01-2026 01:30:00 PM is: On Friday 9th January 2026, the Victoria Big Battery (VBB) was activated under the System Integrity Protection Scheme (SIPS) following unplanned outages of two 330 kV transmission lines (Dederang–Murray No 67 and Dederang–South Morang No 2), starting at 16:05 and lasting until 21:30, with a subsequent non-conformance notice issued by AEMO for unit VBB1 at -250 MW from 20:30 to 21:05; additional unusual charging was observed on Saturday 10th January 2026 between 11:10 and 11:30, though no associated network event was immediately identified. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (intra-day on 9th January 2026, with residual effects on 10th January 2026). Its root_cause is: Unplanned outages of two 330 kV transmission lines (Dederang–Murray No 67 and Dederang–South Morang No 2) in Victoria. Its accident scale is: Two transmission line outages and a non-conformance event for VBB1 at -250 MW. Its dates is: 09-01-2026 (major event) and 10-01-2026 (subsequent charging). Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC1). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely AEMO, grid operators, and consumers relying on system stability). Its cause type is: Technical (transmission line failures) and operational (SIPS activation). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Unplanned outage of Dederang–Murray No 67 330kV line at 16:03 on 9th January; (2) Unplanned outage of Dederang–South Morang No 2 330kV line at 17:00 on 9th January; (3) Activation of SIPS for Victoria Big Battery from 16:05 to 21:30; (4) Non-conformance of VBB1 unit due to inability to meet dispatch target from 20:30 to 21:05.

title: At 17:00 there was an unplanned outage of Dederang – South Morang No2 330kV line
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "At 17:00 there was an unplanned outage of Dederang – South Morang No2 330kV line" posted at 09-01-2026 05:17:00 PM is: AEMO issued an inter-regional transfer limit variation notice on 09/01/2026 after an unplanned outage of the Dederang – South Morang No2 330kV line at 1700 hrs, invoking constraint set V-DBUSS_T at 1720 hrs, which limits transfers on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, impacting Victoria’s grid stability and cross-border power flows. Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of the Dederang – South Morang No2 330kV line. Its accident scale is: Single 330kV transmission line outage (exact capacity not specified). Its dates is: 09/01/2026. Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC) and potentially New South Wales via VIC1-NSW1 interconnector. Its affected key users is: NEM participants, including generators, retailers, and end consumers in Victoria and New South Wales. Its cause type is: Unplanned equipment failure or fault (specific cause not provided). Its causes is: Unplanned outage of Dederang – South Morang No2 330kV line.

title: Violation of (NRM-related) export limits on VIC1-NSW1 on Friday 9th January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Violation of (NRM-related) export limits on VIC1-NSW1 on Friday 9th January 2026" posted at 09-01-2026 05:07:00 PM is: On 9 January 2026, a bushfire triggered the trip of the Dederang–Murray No 67 330kV line, which invoked the 'V-DDMS' constraint set and the 'N^^V_MSDD2' constraint equation, leading to a violation of export limits on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector during the 16:55 dispatch interval (NEM time). Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 09-01-2026 onwards, short-term impact until line restoration. Its root_cause is: Bushfire causing trip of the Dederang–Murray No 67 330kV line. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 09-01-2026 (same as main date). Its affected region is: Victoria and New South Wales (VIC1-NSW1 interconnector region). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural (bushfire). Its causes is: Bushfire; trip of Dederang–Murray No 67 330kV line; invocation of V-DDMS constraint set; activation of N^^V_MSDD2 constraint equation limiting exports.

title: Updates (at 15:35) about Victoria on Friday 9th January 2026.
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Updates (at 15:35) about Victoria on Friday 9th January 2026." posted at 09-01-2026 03:48:00 PM is: On 9 January 2026, Victoria's electricity market faced a reclassification of a non-credible contingency event to a credible one due to lightning strikes on the Yallourn–Rowville 7 and 8 220 kV lines, triggering constraint set V-ROYP78_R_N-2. Additionally, negative residue management constraints were activated on interconnectors VIC1-NSW1 and VIC1-SA1, while temperatures in Geelong began to drop as a weather pattern change set in. The event poses potential grid instability risks given the reclassification of a simultaneous line trip as more likely.

Its relevance level is: Level 1.
Its category is: grid/system instability.
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 09/01/2026 15:25 until further notice.
Its root_cause is: lightning strike.
Its accident scale is: two 220 kV transmission lines (Yallourn–Rowville 7 and 8).
Its dates is: 09-01-2026.
Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC1).
Its affected key users is: Yallourn power station units YWPS1 and YWPS4 (noted as online).
Its cause type is: natural (lightning).
Its causes is: lightning strike on the Yallourn–Rowville 220 kV lines, leading AEMO to reclassify the simultaneous trip of these circuits as a credible contingency.

title: Updates (at 14:30) about Victoria on Friday 9th January 2026.
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Updates (at 14:30) about Victoria on Friday 9th January 2026." posted at 09-01-2026 02:56:00 PM is: On Friday 9th January 2026, Victoria experienced multiple power system events including the tripping of Geelong Moorabool No 1 and 2 220kV lines (non-credible contingency reclassified as credible), reclassification of Dederang – South Morang No 1 and 2 330kV lines as a credible contingency due to bushfire, and invocation of constraint set CA_BRIS_58730C07 to manage inter-regional transfer limits. These network disruptions led to price escalation as many generating units were constrained down or off, creating a challenging day for the grid, especially with bushfires affecting the region. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, starting from 10:55 hrs on 09/01/2026 and ongoing until further notice. Its root_cause is: A combination of an unknown cause for the Geelong Moorabool line trip and a bushfire threatening the Dederang–South Morang lines, leading to reclassification and constraint actions. Its accident scale is: Multiple high-voltage transmission line events (two separate circuits tripped or reclassified) and an interconnector constraint invoked. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 09-01-2026. Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC region). Its affected key users is: Unknown (all electricity consumers in VIC potentially affected). Its cause type is: Combination of unknown technical failure and natural disaster (bushfire). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Unknown cause led to the trip of Geelong Moorabool No 1 & 2 220kV lines at 10:55; 2) Bushfire near Dederang–South Morang prompted AEMO to reclassify the simultaneous trip of those 330kV lines as a credible contingency; 3) To maintain system security, AEMO invoked constraint set CA_BRIS_58730C07 affecting the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector.

title: Friday 9th Jan as a rerun of Wednesday 7th Jan 2026 in Victoria?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Friday 9th Jan as a rerun of Wednesday 7th Jan 2026 in Victoria?" posted at 09-01-2026 12:42:00 PM is: The article highlights that on Friday 9th January 2026, Victoria is once again facing extreme heat and bushfire conditions, closely mirroring the events of Wednesday 7th January 2026, which could lead to electricity market stress and potential disruptions. It provides a snapshot of market monitoring widgets showing similarities between the two days. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, immediate impact on 9th January and potentially into the following days, with echoes of the 7th January event. Its root_cause is: Extreme heat and bushfire conditions causing high electricity demand and possible network instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 09-01-2026 and 07-01-2026. Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Extreme heat, bushfire conditions.

title: ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia exceeds 3,100MW on Thursday 8th January 2026 (a rare event)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Market Demand’ in South Australia exceeds 3,100MW on Thursday 8th January 2026 (a rare event)" posted at 08-01-2026 07:11:00 PM is: On 8 January 2026, extreme heat in Adelaide drove South Australia's 'Market Demand' to 3,111 MW, a rare level exceeding 3,100 MW, though not an all-time record; the high demand challenged AEMO forecasts and was attributed to the heatwave, highlighting the region's sensitivity to temperature-driven electricity consumption. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (intraday, during the peak temperature hours). Its root_cause is: Extreme heat in Adelaide causing a surge in cooling demand. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (a market demand event, not an accident). Its dates is: 08-01-2026. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in South Australia, particularly residential and commercial users relying on air conditioning. Its cause type is: Weather-related (heatwave). Its causes is: High temperatures in Adelaide reaching peak values on 8 January 2026.

title: NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ reaches 32,133MW late Thursday afternoon 8th January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ reaches 32,133MW late Thursday afternoon 8th January 2026" posted at 08-01-2026 06:15:00 PM is: On Thursday 8th January 2026, the NEM-wide Market Demand peaked at 32,133 MW at 17:55 NEM time, with demand in New South Wales and South Australia described as stretched relative to historical ranges but still below all-time maximums. Electricity prices reached nearly $1,000/MWh in South Australia and above $400/MWh in Tasmania, but did not exceed the $1,000/MWh threshold anywhere that evening. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – the day of 8th January 2026. Its root_cause is: Unknown (no specific cause identified in the article). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 08-01-2026. Its affected region is: NEM-wide, with specific mention of New South Wales and South Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all wholesale electricity consumers in the affected regions). Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown.

title: Who’d try to create a ‘better’ price forecast than what the AEMO provides in P5, P30, 7DAY predispatch
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Who’d try to create a ‘better’ price forecast than what the AEMO provides in P5, P30, 7DAY predispatch" posted at 08-01-2026 03:19:00 PM is: This article discusses skepticism about claims of better price forecasts than AEMO's, using a case study from South Australia on 8 January 2026. At the 15:00 dispatch interval, the spot price reached $980.89/MWh, a sharp increase from a forecast of $138.04/MWh just 55 minutes earlier. The spike is attributed to a rapid rise in market demand and a significant unexpected drop in wind generation (UIGF) by 438 MW (40%), highlighting the challenges in forecasting due to variable renewable energy. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (intraday, single dispatch interval). Its root_cause is: Sudden increase in market demand combined with an unexpected 40% drop in wind farm unconstrained availability (UIGF). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 08-01-2026. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA). Its affected key users is: Market participants and consumers exposed to spot prices. Its cause type is: Operational (demand and supply variability). Its causes is: 1) Sharp escalation in market demand in South Australia over the hour before the 15:00 dispatch interval; 2) Unexpected drop in aggregate wind farm UIGF from 1,095 MW to 657 MW (a decline of 438 MW or 40%) within 55 minutes.

title: Spot price volatility later in the evening (Wednesday 7th January 2026)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility later in the evening (Wednesday 7th January 2026)" posted at 07-01-2026 08:29:00 PM is: On the evening of 7 January 2026, spot electricity prices in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) surged above $1,000/MWh after 19:50, with volatility observed in South Australia, Victoria, and Tasmania, driven by high market demand exceeding 33,500 MW NEM-wide and a record 2,956 MW demand in South Australia. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (evening of 7 January 2026). Its root_cause is: high market demand exceeding 33,500 MW NEM-wide and peak demand of 2,956 MW in South Australia. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different) is: 07-01-2026 (same as post date). Its affected region is: South Australia, Victoria, Tasmania (SA, VIC, TAS). Its affected key users is: wholesale electricity market participants. Its cause type is: market supply-demand imbalance. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [high evening demand, likely temperature-driven peak consumption].

title: NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ exceeds 33,500MW on Wednesday evening 7th January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ exceeds 33,500MW on Wednesday evening 7th January 2026" posted at 07-01-2026 06:18:00 PM is: On the evening of 7 January 2026, NEM-wide Market Demand surpassed 33,500 MW, with a peak of 33,583 MW at 17:55, driven by high temperatures and cooling demand, especially in Queensland, which became an "Economic Island" leading to stretched prices despite a 36% reserve margin; a cool change earlier reduced demand in Victoria and NSW, but the overall event reflects a market response to weather-driven load. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (evening of 7 January 2026, lasting several dispatch intervals). Its root_cause is: High ambient temperatures in Queensland and surrounding regions causing peak cooling demand, compounded by Queensland being isolated in an "Economic Island" (limited interconnector capacity limiting imports from southern states). Its accident scale is: Not an accident; a high-demand event with no physical failures. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-01-2026. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, particularly Queensland, with secondary impact on New South Wales and Victoria. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in the NEM, especially residential and commercial users facing higher spot prices. Its cause type is: Weather-driven demand surge and market structural conditions. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) High temperatures leading to increased air-conditioning load, (2) Queensland becoming an "Economic Island" due to limited interconnector capacity, (3) Forecasted high demand 36 hours prior materializing, (4) Cool change in VIC/NSW reducing demand there but not enough to offset NEM-wide peak.

title: Hot weather drives NEM-wide Underlying Demand towards 40,000MW on Wednesday 7th January 2026 … might be highest ever?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Hot weather drives NEM-wide Underlying Demand towards 40,000MW on Wednesday 7th January 2026 … might be highest ever?" posted at 07-01-2026 02:53:00 PM is: The news reports that on 7th January 2026, hot weather in southeastern Australia drove the National Electricity Market (NEM) underlying demand towards 40,000 MW, potentially reaching the highest ever level. The high temperatures are driving increased electricity consumption, likely for cooling. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on 7th January 2026, with potential for sustained high demand if heat continues. Its root_cause is: Hot weather conditions in southeastern Australia. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 07-01-2026. Its affected region is: South-eastern mainland Australia (NEM region). Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in NEM, particularly residential and commercial sectors using air conditioning. Its cause type is: Weather-related (extreme heat). Its causes is: High temperatures leading to increased cooling demand.

title: A quick recap on yesterday evening’s bidding in South Australia with the volatility (Tue 6th Jan 2026)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "A quick recap on yesterday evening’s bidding in South Australia with the volatility (Tue 6th Jan 2026)" posted at 07-01-2026 08:15:00 AM is: On the evening of 6 January 2026, South Australia experienced significant spot price volatility, with prices spiking above $1,000/MWh from 19:20 onwards. This was driven by a combination of factors: solar generation had largely declined (solar 'gone to bed'), wind generation was low throughout the day, and battery storage, which had initially kept prices in check, became depleted. Gas-fired units provided firming longer but were unable to prevent the price spike. Additionally, demand-side resources (negawatts) did not bid. Strong but constrained imports from Victoria also played a role. The event highlights the challenges of the VRE + firming paradigm in South Australia.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Evening of 6 January 2026, specifically from 19:20 ACDT onwards.  
Its root_cause is: Combination of declining solar and low wind generation during evening peak, depletion of battery storage, and insufficient gas-fired generation to prevent price spike.  
Its accident scale is: Not applicable (no physical accident; a market event).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 06-01-2026.  
Its affected region is: South Australia (SA).  
Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in South Australia exposed to spot market prices, particularly those with variable price contracts.  
Its cause type is: Supply shortage due to intermittent renewable generation and limited storage availability.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is:  
- Solar generation declined in the evening.  
- Wind generation was low throughout the day and especially during evening.  
- Battery storage was depleted after initially resisting price pressure.  
- Gas-fired units operated but could not fully offset the deficit.  
- Negawatt (demand response) capacity did not bid.

title: A dose of (high temperatures and) high ‘Market Demand’ on the way this week
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A dose of (high temperatures and) high ‘Market Demand’ on the way this week" posted at 06-01-2026 11:03:00 AM is: AEMO issued local temperature alerts for NSW, SA, and VIC from 6 to 10 January 2026 due to forecast highs reaching 39°C or above, which could increase electricity demand and reduce generator capacity; the article also highlights forecast convergence indicating high market demand on the evenings of 7, 8, and 9 January, urging market participants to review weather impacts on their units. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 06-01-2026 to 10-01-2026. Its root_cause is: High temperature forecasts causing increased electricity demand and potential generator derating. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 06-01-2026 to 10-01-2026. Its affected region is: NSW, SA, VIC. Its affected key users is: Market participants, especially generating units and MNSP converter stations. Its cause type is: Extreme weather (heat wave). Its causes is: Forecast high temperatures exceeding NEM local temperature alert levels; AEMO issued Market Notice MN132519 requesting participants to update available capacity.

title: Large drop in ‘Market Demand’ (amidst oscillation) in SA on Saturday morning 3rd January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Large drop in ‘Market Demand’ (amidst oscillation) in SA on Saturday morning 3rd January 2026" posted at 03-01-2026 08:23:00 AM is: On Saturday morning, 3 January 2026, a 296 MW drop in 'Market Demand' occurred in South Australia over a five-minute period, with oscillations observed. The system frequency remained stable, ruling out grid instability. Potential causes include fluctuations in rooftop solar PV injections, a data glitch, or an aggregated response from invisible embedded Customer Energy Resources, as highlighted in the Nelson Review. The event triggered a notification alert but had no further immediate impact.; Its relevance level is: Level 2; Its category is: market reactions; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: instantaneous (minutes); Its root_cause is: unknown, possibly rooftop PV fluctuations, data glitch, or aggregated response of embedded Customer Energy Resources; Its accident scale is: 296 MW drop in market demand; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 03-01-2026; Its affected region is: South Australia; Its affected key users is: wholesale market participants and NEM operators; Its cause type is: potential data anomaly or renewable generation fluctuation; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: fluctuation in rooftop PV injections, data glitch, aggregated response of embedded Customer Energy Resources.

title: Low, but not that low … ‘Market Demand’ in VIC and SA on Thursday 1st January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low, but not that low … ‘Market Demand’ in VIC and SA on Thursday 1st January 2026" posted at 01-01-2026 01:50:00 PM is: On January 1, 2026, both Victoria and South Australia experienced low 'Market Demand' levels, triggering AEMO Minimum System Load alerts—MSL1 in VIC (demand at 1620 MW, below 1760 MW threshold) and MSL2 in SA (demand at -230 MW, below -117 MW threshold). These conditions indicate an elevated risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state, driven by the New Year's holiday and high contributions from non-scheduled generation (e.g., rooftop solar). The low points, while not all-time minimums, mark a significant operational event in the National Electricity Market.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (hours, from late morning to early afternoon on 01-01-2026). Its root_cause is: Insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state due to low operational load on a public holiday combined with high output from non-scheduled renewable generation (e.g., rooftop solar). Its accident scale is: Not an accident but a system condition; scale is regional affecting two states (VIC and SA). Its dates is: 01-01-2026. Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC) and South Australia (SA). Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants (generators, retailers), and potentially residential/commercial consumers via price impacts. Its cause type is: Operational (low demand). Its causes is: [Low demand due to New Year's Day holiday, High contributions from non-scheduled generation (rooftop solar), Supplementary data: Forecast operational demand excluding significant non-scheduled generation fell below minimum system load thresholds].
